dimensions.name: Construction Methods
dimensions.description: System selection, buildability, and construction feasibility
generalGuidance: Always consider how a design will actually be built - structurally compliant designs can still be impractical to construct
Where conventional construction methods are constrained, investigate alternative systems early - changes are least costly at the design stage

items.name: Off-form concrete upturns near obstructions

data.question: When designing concrete upturns near obstructions, what clearance is required for conventional formwork, and at what point should alternative systems be considered?
data.parameter: Available clearance between upturn face and nearest obstruction (mm)
data.background: Conventional off-form concrete upturns require practical access to both sides of the formwork for placement, bracing, tying, bolt-up, stripping and inspection. This assumption breaks down when upturns are positioned close to existing fences, retaining walls, boundaries or other obstructions. It would be unwise to assume that plywood pressed against an obstruction can adequately restrain wet concrete pressure, particularly for slender upturns once heights exceed approximately 600 mm. Where access to either side is limited, the designer should consider whether conventional off-form construction is feasible before committing to an off-form concrete design.

thresholds.guidance: Full access for formwork placement, bracing, bolt-up, and stripping. Both coil tie and threaded bar systems feasible.
Coil tie system feasible but access is tight — a person cannot comfortably work behind the upturn. Consider whether alternatives would be simpler.
Coil ties cannot be used (plywood + timbers + bolt = 700 mm minimum). Threaded bar only, tightened by reaching in at arm's length. Only for upturns/walls up to ~1200 mm high.

Conventional double-sided off-form construction is unlikely to be feasible without redesign, specialist one-sided forming, temporary access from the adjacent side, or an alternative construction system.

alternatives.title: Dincel or similar permanent formwork
alternatives.description: Permanent formwork systems can allow construction where rear-side access is not available because the formwork remains in place and does not require conventional stripping from the rear side. These systems can be rendered or finished with an acrylic coating to achieve a concrete-like appearance where required by the client.

alternatives.title: Blockwork construction
alternatives.description: Blockwork may be structurally suitable depending on the wall function and design requirements. The engineer must verify structural adequacy, durability and any retaining or load-transfer requirements. Blockwork can also be rendered to match a concrete finish where aesthetics are important.

alternatives.title: Reposition the wall forward
alternatives.description: The wall or upturn may be moved forward to provide adequate working clearance. This is usually only viable where the client brief, boundary constraints, architectural layout and usable floor area allow it, as walls are often placed close to boundaries for a specific reason.

alternatives.title: Precast or tilt-up panels
alternatives.description: Precast or tilt-up construction may avoid the rear-side formwork access issue, but its suitability depends on project scale, crane access, site logistics and panel geometry. For larger projects with crane access this may be feasible; for smaller projects without a crane, an in-situ or permanent formwork solution may be more realistic.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to reinforced concrete upturns and walls close to a boundary, existing structure, fence or other obstruction, where conventional off-form construction may require access to both sides of the element.

checks.title: Upturn height and length
checks.description: A small nib and a taller wall are not the same construction problem. Taller or longer elements increase bracing demand, concrete pressure, access requirements and sequencing constraints.

checks.title: Adjacent land access
checks.description: If the obstruction is neighbouring land and access is possible and practically available and required permissions are received, conventional formwork may remain possible. Where access is not available, even moderate clearances may be insufficient for bracing, bolt-up, stripping or inspection.

checks.title: Required final finish
checks.description: If an off-form concrete appearance is required, alternatives such as permanent PVC formwork or blockwork may require render, acrylic coating, applied finish or architectural acceptance.

checks.title: Project scale and repetition
checks.description: Specialist systems, precast or tilt-up options may be more practical on larger or repetitive projects than for a short isolated upturn.

checks.title: Regional availability
checks.description: Permanent formwork systems, blockwork labour, precast suppliers, crane access and common site practices vary by region. The location tag should be considered before applying the guidance elsewhere.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability review prompt, not as a blanket prohibition on the design detail. The appropriate response is to confirm the construction method early.

data.evidence:
Conventional formwork requires rear clearance for bracing and bolt-up access. The thresholds are derived from the physical components and working envelopes required for each tie system, cross-referenced with formwork literature (Hurd, 2005; Jha, 2012) and industry consultation.

700 mm threshold (coil tie minimum)
Plywood sheathing (18 mm) + studs and double wales (196 mm) + coil bolt length (310 mm) + hand and tool clearance (~127 mm) + working buffer (~50 mm) = ~700 mm. Below this clearance a coil tie cannot be installed and tightened.

350 mm threshold (threaded Z-bar minimum)
Plywood sheathing (18 mm) + studs and double wales (196 mm) + nut and space to work it on and off (~100 mm, approximately twice nut height to allow the nut to be threaded on and off) = ~314 mm, rounded up to 350 mm to allow practical tolerance on site. The hand drops out of the breakdown because it occupies the same envelope as the nut being tightened, and the working buffer drops out because the operator reaches in at arm's length rather than standing behind the formwork. Below 350 mm a threaded Z-bar cannot be installed and tightened from the rear side.

1000 mm threshold (full working access)
700 mm (coil tie components installed) + ~300 mm working envelope for a person to stand and work behind the formwork = ~1000 mm. Above this clearance both tie systems are feasible and the operator has comfortable working room for bracing, bolt-up, stripping and inspection.

A concrete upturn wall designed as off-form concrete near a site boundary was found to be infeasible during construction due to zero clearance behind the wall and no access to adjacent land. The design was revised to a Dincel permanent formwork system.

References
Dincel Structural Walling. (2026). Construction Manual. Dincel Structural Walling.
Hurd, M. K. (2005). Formwork for Concrete (7th ed.). American Concrete Institute.
Jha, K. N. (2012). Formwork for Concrete Structures. Tata McGraw Hill Education Private Limited.

examples.caption: Original design: off-form concrete upturn specified directly against the site boundary. No clearance behind for conventional formwork bracing or bolt-up access.
Dincel (PVC permanent formwork) system installed to eliminate the requirement for rear bracing in a wall 1000 mm high.

items.name: Conventional RC vs post-tensioned: formwork cycling on repeated floors

data.question: On a multi-storey building with repeated suspended floors, has the construction-cycle and formwork-quantity advantage of post-tensioning been weighed in the slab system choice — or has the choice between conventional RC and post-tensioned been made on structural grounds (spans, depth) alone?
data.parameter: Number of repeated suspended floors over which the slab system (conventional RC or post-tensioned) will be cycled.
data.background:
The choice between a conventionally reinforced (RC) slab and a post-tensioned (PT) slab is usually discussed in structural terms — span, depth, deflection and load. This entry is not about those structural drivers, which remain controlling. It is about a construction-method consequence of the choice that is easy to overlook at design stage: how the slab system affects the rate at which formwork can be cycled up a multi-storey building, and how much formwork has to be on site to do it.

Multi-storey concrete construction works by cycling a limited number of formwork and shoring sets up the building, rather than buying a new set for every floor. The faster each floor's forms can be released and lifted to the floor above, the fewer sets are needed and the faster the structure rises. The key point is that what is released early is the soffit formwork and plywood — the expensive, reusable forming surface — while the back-props (reshores) stay in place and carry construction load down through several slabs below. Back-propping for an extended period is normal and acceptable; the constructability gain comes from freeing the forms, not from removing the shoring.

A post-tensioned slab supports this earlier release. With draped tendons, stressing balances part of the slab's self-weight, which unloads the forms and shores supporting it and produces a load distribution through the temporary works that is different from a conventionally reinforced slab. Kajewski (1998), measuring shore loads on a real multi-level PT building, found that this allows the slab pour cycle to be shortened and makes PT slabs well suited to reshoring. In the scenario modelled, the cycle could be reduced from 7 days to 4 days on the factor-of-safety criterion alone (a saving of 3 days per slab), but because PT slabs carry very little conventional reinforcement they can be damaged if loaded by the slab above before they have been stressed. Allowing each slab to be stressed before it is loaded limits the practical cycle to 5 days — still a saving of about 2 days per slab. The practical figure to design around is therefore approximately 2 days per slab (conservative), with up to 3 days achievable where earlier stressing is possible.

On a single suspended floor a 2-day saving is minor and would rarely drive the system choice. The point of this entry is that the saving repeats on every floor: across many floors it compounds into a significant programme saving, and the reduced number of formwork sets needed to maintain the cycle is itself a cost and logistics benefit. The more the floor plate repeats up the building, the more the construction-cycle and formwork-quantity advantage of PT should be weighed alongside the structural factors — not treated as an afterthought once the structural system has already been fixed.

thresholds.guidance: A single suspended floor. The cycle saving (~2 days) does not repeat, so it rarely drives the slab system choice on its own. Decide on structural, cost and other project grounds; the construction-cycle advantage of PT is not a significant factor at this scale.

The saving begins to repeat (~4-6 days over the project). Usually still not decisive on its own, but worth weighing where the programme is tight or formwork availability is constrained. Consider the cycle-time and formwork-quantity effect as one input to the system choice.

The cumulative saving (~8-10 days) is now a real construction-method question, not a side issue. The formwork-cycle efficiency and the reduced number of formwork sets required should be actively reviewed as part of the slab system selection, alongside the structural drivers.

Across this many floors the saving compounds to ~12+ days and the reduction in formwork material becomes substantial. There is a strong constructability case to weigh post-tensioning for cycle-time and formwork-quantity reasons. This does not mean PT must be used — structural, cost, fire and durability factors may still govern — but the construction-method consequence should be a deliberate, documented part of the decision.

workedExample.title: Worked example — how the saving compounds
workedExample.caveat: Based on Kajewski's (1998) modelled saving of approximately 2 days per slab (conservative; the factor-of-safety-only case gives up to 3 days per slab). These figures are from one modelled scenario (a 7-day baseline cycle with 3 levels of undisturbed shoring). The actual saving on any project depends on the baseline cycle, shoring arrangement, concrete strengths and stressing schedule. Use the table to see how a per-floor saving scales, not as a fixed prediction.
workedExample.headline: A modest per-floor saving becomes a significant programme effect once the floor plate repeats up the building.
tableHeaders: What it means for the system choice
tableRows: Rarely enough to drive the choice on its own
Marginal — only counts if the programme is tight
Starting to matter; weigh cycle time in the decision
Cycle-time efficiency should be actively reviewed
Strong case to weigh PT for cycle and formwork-quantity reasons
workedExample.howToUse: Count the suspended floors over which the slab system will repeat, read across to the approximate cumulative saving, and use it to judge how heavily the construction-cycle advantage should weigh against the structural and cost factors that also govern the choice. The saving is a programme effect; the reduced number of formwork sets needed to sustain the cycle is a parallel cost and logistics benefit.
assumptions: Per-floor saving of ~2 days is Kajewski's (1998) preferred-practice figure, which allows each PT slab to be stressed before it is loaded by the slab above (limiting the cycle to 5 days against a 7-day conventional baseline).

The factor-of-safety-only case gives a 4-day cycle (~3 days/slab saving), but risks damaging the lightly reinforced PT slab if it is loaded before stressing; the 2-day figure is therefore the conservative, recommended basis.

The cumulative column simply multiplies ~2 days by the number of repeated floors; it assumes the saving is realised on each floor, which depends on the cycle being formwork-limited rather than constrained by other trades or program logic.
Figures are from Kajewski's modelled scenario (7-day baseline, 3 levels undisturbed shoring) and will differ with a different baseline cycle, shoring depth, concrete strength or stressing schedule.
workedExample.assumptionsNote: The direction of the effect — saving grows with repeated floors — is well supported. The exact per-floor figure is scenario-specific and should be confirmed against the project's own cycle and shoring arrangement.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to multi-storey reinforced concrete buildings with repeated suspended floors, where a limited number of formwork and shoring sets are cycled up the structure and the designer is choosing between a conventionally reinforced and a post-tensioned slab system.

checks.title: This entry covers only the construction-cycle dimension of the choice
checks.description: The advantage described here is fundamentally about formwork cycling, and it only becomes significant when the floor plate repeats up a building. On a small or low-rise job, or where the saving does not repeat enough times to matter, conventional reinforcement may well remain the right choice. The broader RC-versus-PT decision is also governed by span, slab depth, deflection, load, cost, fire resistance, durability and future penetrations — all outside this entry's scope, and any of which may override the cycle argument. This entry addresses only the formwork-cycle and formwork-quantity consequence; it is one input to weigh, not the whole decision.

checks.title: Post-tensioning must itself be feasible and available
checks.description: The cycle saving is irrelevant if PT cannot practically be built on the project. The tendons must be able to be stressed — that needs access to the anchorages at the slab edges and room to operate the stressing jack, which can be a problem against boundaries, adjacent structures or congested edges. PT also depends on a local PT supply chain: tendons, anchorages, stressing equipment and an experienced PT subcontractor. In regions or on projects where PT contractors are not readily available, or where the edges cannot be stressed, conventional reinforcement may be the practical choice regardless of how many floors are involved. Confirm PT is buildable and available for this project before letting the cycle saving drive the decision.

checks.title: It is the forms that cycle, not the shoring that disappears
checks.description: The saving comes from releasing the soffit forms and plywood early so they can be lifted to the floor above, while back-props (reshores) remain and carry load down through several slabs. Extended back-propping is normal and is not the constraint; the gain is in freeing the reusable forming surface sooner. The shoring/reshoring system must still be designed to carry the multi-level construction loads.

checks.title: Early loading of unstressed PT slabs
checks.description: PT slabs contain very little conventional reinforcement and can be damaged if loaded by the slab above before they have been stressed. The conservative ~2 day/slab saving assumes each slab is stressed before it is loaded. Pushing to the larger saving by stressing earlier may require higher early-strength concrete to avoid anchorage failure during stressing.

checks.title: Early-stripping formwork systems are an alternative route to fast cycles
checks.description: Modern drophead (early-stripping) formwork systems allow conventional slab forms to be released early too, by leaving the drophead props in place while the panels are struck. This means PT is not the only way to achieve a fast formwork cycle. PT's distinctive benefit is that it also reduces the reshoring demand and the quantity of formwork required, but the system choice should consider what the contractor's formwork system can already achieve.

checks.title: The saving must actually be on the critical path
checks.description: A faster formwork cycle only shortens the programme if the structure cycle is the binding constraint. Where other trades, procurement, or program logic govern the floor cycle, the theoretical per-floor saving may not be fully realised. Confirm that the cycle is formwork-limited before relying on the cumulative figure.

checks.title: Regional and contractor practice
checks.description: PT availability, contractor experience, formwork systems and typical cycle times vary by region and contractor. The cited figures are from an Australian study; confirm the baseline cycle and achievable saving with the contractor for the specific project.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a prompt to weigh the construction-cycle and formwork-quantity effect in the slab system decision at design stage, not as a rule that PT must be used above a particular number of floors. The advantage comes down to formwork cycling and only counts when floors repeat; it must also be set against the structural and cost drivers and against whether PT is itself feasible and available. The intent is that on a repetitive multi-storey building this construction-method consequence is a deliberate part of the decision, rather than the system being chosen on span and depth alone and the programme implications discovered later.

alternatives.title: Decide the slab system with the floor-count saving counted in, before the design is committed
alternatives.description: On a repetitive multi-storey building the system choice is the designer's to make at design stage, and it is usually locked in well before a contractor is appointed. So the formwork-cycle effect has to be weighed as part of that decision, not deferred. Estimate the saving per floor against a realistic baseline cycle, multiply it across the number of floors, and add the parallel benefit of needing fewer formwork and shoring sets to sustain the cycle. The more the floor plate repeats, the more strongly this should pull the decision toward post-tensioning — so that on a tall, repetitive building the construction-cycle advantage is a deliberate reason to design it PT, weighed against the structural, cost and feasibility factors, rather than a benefit noticed only after the system was already chosen on span and depth.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by Australian academic research with on-site measurement, a peer-reviewed journal paper, and industry sources, together with the established mechanics of multi-level formwork cycling. The direction of the effect — that post-tensioning shortens the formwork cycle and that the saving compounds across repeated floors — is well supported. The specific per-floor figure is taken from one modelled scenario and is treated as indicative rather than universal.

Primary source — shore-load measurement. Kajewski (1998) measured actual shore loads during the construction of a multi-level post-tensioned building and modelled the multi-level formwork load distribution. The research found that the improved factors of safety with PT slabs allow a shortening of the slab construction cycle time, that the low level of load redistribution during stripping makes PT slabs well suited to reshoring, and that PT construction allows a reduction in the quantity of formwork materials required. In the scenario presented (7-day baseline cycle, three levels of undisturbed shoring), the cycle could be reduced to 4 days on the factor-of-safety criterion alone — a saving of 3 days per slab — but because lightly reinforced PT slabs can be damaged if loaded before stressing, a 5-day cycle that allows each slab to be stressed before it is loaded is preferable, still saving about 2 days per slab. This is the basis for the conservative ~2 day/slab figure used in this entry.

Peer-reviewed corroboration. The same body of work was published as Kajewski's analysis of multilevel formwork load distribution with post-tensioned slabs in the Journal of Construction Engineering and Management, presenting design methods for the multi-level formwork process validated against the measured shore loads.

Mechanism. With draped tendons, post-tensioning balances part of the slab self-weight, unloading the forms and shores supporting the slab by the balanced amount and producing a load distribution through the temporary works different from a conventionally reinforced slab. This is what allows the soffit forms to be released earlier while the reshores remain.

Industry sources. Post-tensioning system suppliers and formwork literature consistently list earlier striking of formwork, reduced back-propping requirements and faster formwork reuse among the construction benefits of post-tensioned slabs, consistent with the measured findings above. These are industry/commercial sources and are used as corroboration of the direction of the effect rather than for specific figures.

Limitations of the figure. The ~2-3 day per-slab saving is from a single modelled scenario with a specific baseline cycle and shoring arrangement. Actual savings depend on the project's baseline cycle, shoring depth, concrete strengths, stressing schedule and whether the formwork cycle is the binding constraint on the programme. The cumulative figures in the worked example assume the saving is realised on every floor. The thresholds should be read as a guide to how the effect scales, not as a precise prediction.

References
Kajewski, S. L. (1998). Post-tensioning and its effect on multi-level formwork load distribution (PhD thesis). Queensland University of Technology.
Kajewski, S. L. (2005). Multilevel formwork load distribution with post-tensioned slabs. Journal of Construction Engineering and Management, 131(2).

supportingImages.title:
Why post-tensioning unloads the formwork — Kajewski (2005)
This figure is included because it shows the mechanism the whole entry depends on: why a PT slab can release its forms earlier than a conventional one. In a freshly cast slab (a), the formwork and shoring carry essentially the entire self-weight — a load ratio of 1.0 in the shores and 0.0 in the slab. When draped tendons are stressed, the post-tensioning balances part of the self-weight and the slab lifts to carry that portion itself, unloading the shores by the balanced amount: at full balancing (b) the ratio is 1.0 in the slab and 0.0 in the shores, and at 80% balancing (c) it is 0.8 in the slab and 0.2 in the shores. That unloading of the temporary works is what lets the soffit forms be struck and cycled to the floor above sooner, while the reshores stay in place — the source of the per-floor saving in this entry.

evidenceSupport.summary: The post-tensioning effect on formwork cycling is well supported by measured shore-load research (Kajewski) and a peer-reviewed paper, but the per-floor saving comes from a single modelled scenario and the floor-count bands are a reasoned judgement about when the compounding saving becomes material, so the thresholds a user acts on are indicative rather than evidenced.

items.name: Optional pour breaks to keep concrete trades flowing
items._status: REBUILT — parameter shortened; background rewritten around optional pour breaks, crew continuity and formwork crew remobilisation; Löfgren man-hour image added.

data.question: On repetitive suspended concrete, has an optional pour-break (construction-joint) location been shown and detailed at design stage, so the contractor can stage the work and keep the forming and fixing crews moving — rather than the structure defaulting to a single continuous pour because a break was never offered?
data.parameter: Whether the design enables an optional pour break where it would help the programme.
data.background:
Once the groundworks are complete, a suspended concrete structure is usually built through three repeating site activities: formwork, reinforcement fixing and concrete placing. These activities do not behave the same way. Formwork and reinforcement fixing benefit from continuous flow: the crew finishes one zone, moves to the next, and keeps repeating the same operation. Concrete placing is different. It happens in short bursts, then the concrete has to gain enough strength before the next dependent work can proceed.

The constructability problem arises where a large floor or repeated area is treated as one single pour. The formwork and reinforcement crews may complete their work, but then have no next zone available while the concrete is placed, cures and is stripped. A crew with no work in front of it will not necessarily wait on site. The formwork crew may be moved to another job, and once that happens the programme is no longer controlled only by when the concrete is ready — it also depends on when that crew can return. That creates waiting, remobilisation and sequencing risk, and it matters because formwork is usually the largest labour component of an in-situ concrete structure.

An optional pour break gives the contractor a planned way to split the floor or repeated area into zones. While one zone is being poured or gaining strength, the formwork crew can move ahead to the next zone and the reinforcement crew can follow. The concrete placing crew still comes and goes in bursts, but the forming and fixing work is no longer forced to stop behind one large pour. This is the programme logic behind the entry: the design does not need to dictate the final pour sequence, but it should not remove a sensible staging option where the work clearly repeats.

This is only useful where there is somewhere for the crews to go. On repeated floors, repeated bays or a large floor plate that can sensibly be divided into zones, an optional pour break may help maintain crew continuity. On a small one-off slab, an isolated area, or work that does not repeat, a break may add a construction joint without creating any real programme benefit. In those cases the contractor should usually be left to pour the work in the most practical way.

The design issue is that a pour break is also a construction joint. It cannot be treated as a casual site decision if reinforcement continuity, shear transfer, durability, watertightness or crack control are affected. If no acceptable break location is shown, the contractor may either request one during construction, when the programme is already under pressure, or simply assume that no break is available and price/build the work as one continuous pour. Showing an optional pour-break location during design keeps the decision open: the contractor can stage the work if it helps the programme, or pour through if that is simpler, but the joint location and detail have already been considered by the engineer.

thresholds.guidance: On repetitive suspended concrete where the trades could flow, the design shows an optional pour-break location, clearly marked as optional, on a structurally sound plane, with a construction-joint detail referenced (continuity reinforcement; waterstop where the element is wet). The contractor can stage the work — keeping formworkers and steelfixers moving through zones while each pour cures — or pour through. The decision has been made with the design open, so no one needs to return to the engineer for it.

The work repeats enough that staging could keep the trades flowing, but no optional break is shown. Because a structural break needs the engineer's sign-off, this resolves one of two ways: the contractor raises it mid-job and the joint is designed reactively under programme pressure, or — more often — the contractor assumes it is unavailable and builds one continuous pour, and the programme benefit is silently lost. Consider showing an optional break before issue. One-off or small non-repetitive work sits outside this entry — leave it to the contractor and do not add unnecessary breaks.

alternatives.title: Show an optional pour-break location, with a detail
alternatives.description: On repetitive suspended concrete, mark a buildable pour-break (construction-joint) location on the relevant plan, clearly labelled as optional, and reference a typical joint detail: continuity reinforcement, a sound joint plane, and a waterstop where the element is watertight. Doing this while the design is open lets the engineer place the break advantageously and lets the contractor choose to stage the work and run the trades continuously — without having to come back for a sign-off mid-construction. Do not add breaks to one-off or small non-repetitive work, where the contractor is better placed to decide.

applicability.appliesWhere: Repetitive reinforced-concrete suspended structures (multiple similar floors, bays or zones) where the number and staging of pours can be influenced at design stage, and where keeping the forming and fixing trades working continuously would shorten the programme. It concerns whether an optional pour break is enabled — not the structural detailing of the joint itself, nor crack-control (contraction-joint) spacing, which are separate matters. One-off or small non-repetitive work is out of scope.

checks.title: Does the work actually repeat?
checks.description: Count the substantially similar pour cycles (floors, bays, zones). The benefit exists only where a freed-up crew has matching work to flow into. Low repetition means there is nothing downstream to move to, so leave the work whole.

checks.title: Which trades are you keeping continuous?
checks.description: Forming and fixing are the activities worth keeping continuous; placing is discontinuous by nature and comes in bursts regardless. Formwork is also the largest single share of the labour, so retaining the formwork crew is the main prize.

checks.title: Is the break location sound and buildable?
checks.description: An optional break is only useful if it sits on a structurally sound plane with workable access and a sensible detail. A break shown in a poor location is not a usable option.

checks.title: Watertightness
checks.description: Where the break falls in a watertight element it becomes a potential leakage path; locate it deliberately and detail a waterstop. This is the main case where the break location genuinely matters structurally.

checks.title: Regional joint detail
checks.description: Construction-joint detailing (continuity steel, waterstop, surface preparation) varies by region and specification. This entry concerns the decision to enable a break, not the local detailing of the joint.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability prompt at design stage, not a requirement to split. The aim is to make a beneficial option available where the work repeats — and to avoid adding unnecessary breaks where it does not.

data.evidence:
Workflow and zoning (the mechanism). Takt and location-based planning improve construction workflow by breaking repetitive work into zones so trades can flow through the work rather than queue behind one large activity. Frandson (2019) shows this programme logic in Figure 9-4: for a sequence of repeating activities, increasing the number of zones reduces the overall time to complete the sequence, with the reduction greatest where many activities follow one another. This supports the staging logic of the entry, but it is not a concrete-placement rule and does not set a maximum pour size.

Formwork dominates the labour. Löfgren et al. (2001) provide a man-hour breakdown for concrete structures showing formwork as the dominant labour activity: approximately 49% of the man-hours, compared with 22% for reinforcement and 11% for concrete operations. This supports the entry’s focus on keeping the forming and fixing crews flowing, rather than treating the concrete placing crew as the main programme driver.

Crew continuity. The practical programme risk is that a specialist crew with no next zone available may be moved to another site. Once that happens, the programme depends not only on when the concrete is ready, but on when the crew can return. An optional pour break can reduce this risk where the work repeats by giving the formwork and reinforcement crews a planned next zone to move into while earlier concrete gains strength.

Scope of the evidence. The evidence supports the programme and workflow logic of planned staging. It does not replace concrete-specific guidance on construction-joint design, reinforcement continuity, shear transfer, waterstops, cold joints, concrete setting time or placement limits. Those matters must still be designed and specified separately. The rating is therefore a constructability prompt: where repetitive concrete work would benefit from staging, the design should make an acceptable optional break available rather than leaving the issue to be raised under construction pressure.

References
Frandson, A. (2019). Takt Time Planning as a Work Structuring Method to Improve Construction Work Flow. PhD dissertation, University of California, Berkeley (Figure 9-4).
Löfgren, I., Gylltoft, K., & Kutti, T. (2001). In-situ concrete building – Innovations in Formwork. Department of Structural Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology / Thomas Concrete Group.

supportingImages.title:
Löfgren et al. (2001) — concrete structure man-hour breakdown
Approximate relative breakdown of man-hours for a concrete structure. Formwork accounts for about 49% of the work and reinforcement about 22%, compared with concrete operations at about 11%. This supports the entry’s focus on keeping forming and fixing crews flowing, rather than treating the concrete placing crew as the main programme driver.

Frandson (2019), Figure 9-4 — effect of the number of zones on sequence duration
The time to complete a repeating activity sequence falls sharply as the work is split into more zones, and the gain grows with the number of activities in the sequence. It supports the programme logic behind staging a concrete pour into zones — keeping the forming and fixing trades flowing — rather than any concrete-placement rule. Reproduced from A. Frandson, Takt Time Planning as a Work Structuring Method to Improve Construction Work Flow, PhD dissertation, UC Berkeley, 2019.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by workflow literature and labour-breakdown data, but the threshold bands rely partly on practical programme logic and would benefit from further project examples or regional feedback.
