items.name: Curing shortfall in suspended slabs and beams
items._status: REVIEWED — references corrected and wording tightened; available entry.

data.question: When the design relies on the specified compressive strength of a thin suspended slab or beam, has the curing shortfall that commonly occurs on site been allowed for — by designing one standard grade below the grade specified for supply — or is the full specified grade assumed to arrive regardless?
data.parameter: For shallow elements (suspended slabs and beams), the compressive strength relied upon in design relative to the specified grade: where effective curing is not verified, design at the standard grade one step below the grade specified and called up for supply.
data.background:
The specified compressive strength is verified using standard-cured cylinders, not by assuming that the in-place slab or beam has experienced the same curing environment. The strength that ends up in the structure is affected by how the concrete is placed, compacted and — critically — cured under site conditions.

For suspended slabs and beams this gap is at its widest. These are shallow elements: the unformed top surface is a large fraction of their total surface area, so they lose moisture readily and depend heavily on curing to reach their potential strength. Walls and columns, with far less exposed surface relative to volume, are much less sensitive.

The practical problem is that curing of these elements is often inadequate or unverified. Suspended slabs are rarely ponded or continuously water-cured; at best a compound or sealed sheeting is applied, and in many cases curing is brief or not deliberately carried out. Industry guidance and regulator observations both identify this as a real site-practice issue (see Evidence Basis). So the realistic design question for a thin slab or beam is not simply ‘what grade is specified?’ but ‘what strength is likely to be present if curing is poor?’

ACI 214.4R reports that cores from poorly cured shallow elements average 77% of companion cores from properly cured elements. The constructability response is to stop assuming the full specified strength will arrive, and instead design the element at the strength it will realistically reach where curing is not verified. Conveniently, the standard grade ladder steps down by almost exactly this penalty, so the practical rule is: design one standard grade below the grade you specify and call up for supply — specify 40 and design 32; specify 32 and design 25.

This costs very little where it does not matter and protects the designer where it does. A slab’s bending capacity is usually governed by the reinforcement, so the lower grade barely changes it. The penalty bites instead in punching shear and deflection — both of which scale with the square root of strength — and those are exactly the checks that govern thin flat plates and flat slabs. Relying on 40 while the slab realistically delivers around 31 does not necessarily mean the slab fails; it means the margin believed to exist is quietly eroded. On a punching- or deflection-governed element, that margin is what is protecting the design.

This is why the issue belongs in a constructability framework, not only in a compliance discussion. If the shortfall surfaces after the slab is built and occupied, it can appear as cracked floor finishes, cracked toppings or tiles, excessive deflection, ponding, serviceability complaints, or a punching-shear concern requiring structural assessment. Those outcomes create cost, disruption, investigation, sequencing problems and possible remediation work after construction, when the options are far more limited. Designing one grade down, or verifying curing when the full grade is relied upon, is a low-cost design-stage control against a potentially high-cost construction and post-construction problem.

thresholds.guidance: On suspended slabs and beams, the design uses the standard grade one step below the specified grade (e.g. specify 40, design 32), and that reduced grade is carried through the punching-shear and deflection checks. The specified grade is still shown on the drawings for supply, durability and acceptance. This is the default best-practice position where effective curing is not verified.

Full specified strength is used in design because the curing needed to achieve it is deliberately specified, suitable for the element, and verified as a hold point, or because the element is not strength-critical and has generous punching and deflection margins. Acceptable — but the designer has deliberately chosen to rely on curing or spare margin rather than the one-grade-down hedge.

The full specified strength is relied upon on a punching- or deflection-governed thin flat plate or flat slab, with no curing assurance and no allowance for the realistic shortfall. The design is leaning on strength that may not be present in the structure.

As above, and the governing check is already tight at convergence — a strength-dependent reduction of around 11–12% would push punching shear or deflection past its limit. Either design one grade down, or make effective curing a documented, verified hold point before relying on the full grade.

workedExample.title: Strength relied upon vs. specified — shallow elements
workedExample.caveat: Applies to poorly cured shallow elements (suspended slabs and beams). The 0.77 factor is a measured average across studies, not a precise prediction for a given pour — see Applicability and Limitations.
workedExample.headline: Poorly cured shallow elements average 77% of the strength of properly cured ones (ACI 214.4R). The standard grade ladder steps down by almost the same amount, so design one grade below what you specify:
workedExample.howToUse: Keep specifying the grade everyone expects (commonly 40 MPa for suspended slabs), so supply and acceptance are unaffected and nothing on the drawings looks unusual. In the structural model, analyse the shallow elements at the next grade down. The adopted grade is the nearest standard step at or below the 0.77 figure, so it captures roughly a 20–22% reduction; the last couple of percent sits within the general design margins.
assumptions: 0.77 factor: ACI 214.4R reports that cores from poorly cured shallow elements average 77% of companion cores from properly cured elements, across ages of 28, 56, 91 and 365 days, citing Bartlett and MacGregor (1996).
Adopted grade = the nearest standard grade at or below the realistic in-place figure, chosen because it already exists in design software and on the supply schedule.
workedExample.assumptionsNote: This factor is specific to shallow elements (slabs and beams). ACI 214.4R reports that walls and columns lose only about 10% to poor curing, so they must not be derated this way.

applicability.appliesWhere: Suspended slabs and beams — shallow elements with a large unformed surface area relative to volume — whose design relies on the specified compressive strength, and where proper curing is unlikely to be carried out on site.

checks.title: This is the poorly-cured case, and verified curing is the escape hatch
checks.description: The 23% penalty is the gap between poorly cured and properly cured shallow elements. If effective curing (for example a suitable curing compound, sealed sheeting, retained formwork or another project-specific method) is specified, suitable for the element and genuinely verified, the element can be designed at its full specified grade. The derating is the price of assuming curing will be absent, brief or unverified; assuring curing buys the strength back.

checks.title: Apply to shallow elements only, not walls or columns
checks.description: The same source shows walls and columns lose only about 10% to poor curing, because their exposed surface is a small fraction of their volume. Do not knock a grade off a column on this basis — it is a slab-and-beam rule.

checks.title: Carry the reduced grade through punching and deflection, not just flexure
checks.description: Bending capacity is reinforcement-governed and barely moves with concrete grade. Punching-shear capacity and elastic modulus both scale with √f′<sub>c</sub> (about an 11–12% reduction going from 40 to 32), and poor curing can also increase creep and shrinkage. The reduced grade matters most precisely where thin flat slabs are usually governed.

checks.title: Blended (low-carbon) mixes are more curing-sensitive
checks.description: Fly-ash and slag mixes depend on sustained curing more than plain cement, so the assumption that curing will not happen is more consequential, not less, as binders get greener. The 0.77 is a reasonable central figure, but the real penalty for a given pour depends on the mix, the weather, the element geometry and how much curing actually occurred.

applicability.note: Keep specifying the grade required for the design intent and durability on the drawings — this rule changes the strength you analyse with where curing is unverified, not the grade you call up for supply and acceptance. It is a deliberate design conservatism, not a substitute for specifying or pursuing proper curing.

alternatives.title: Design shallow strength-critical elements one grade down
alternatives.description: For suspended slabs and beams where punching or deflection governs, analyse at the standard grade one step below the specified grade (specify 40, design 32; specify 32, design 25). Keep the specified grade on the drawings for supply, durability and acceptance.

alternatives.title: Use the full grade only where curing is assured
alternatives.description: If the full specified grade is relied upon, make curing a deliberate and verifiable construction requirement. Specify a curing method that will realistically be executed on a suspended slab, such as a curing compound applied promptly, sealed sheeting, retained formwork or another suitable method, and require it as a documented hold point. Verified curing returns the element to the properly cured case and justifies using the full specified strength.

data.evidence:
Magnitude of the penalty (research-backed). ACI 214.4R-03, Guide for Obtaining Cores and Interpreting Compressive Strength Results, reports that the in-place strength of slabs and beams is more sensitive to curing than that of walls or columns, because the unformed top surface is a large fraction of the total surface area. Drawing on four studies, it states that cores from poorly cured shallow elements average 77% of companion cores from properly cured elements, consistently at ages of 28, 56, 91 and 365 days, citing Bartlett and MacGregor (1996). The same source notes that the strength loss for walls and columns is only about 10%. The 23% figure used here is therefore measured field data on the element type this entry covers — not an acceptance threshold and not a general reduction for all concrete elements.

That the penalty may actually be incurred (qualitative, but from authoritative sources). Cement Concrete & Aggregates Australia states that, in practice, concrete is often not purposefully cured or is cured for only a very short period, and calls this poor practice that stops the concrete reaching its full strength and durability. Access Canberra building inspectors reported curing problems on several ACT building sites, specifically floor slabs both on grade and suspended, and noted that in many cases builders were unaware of the curing requirements shown on the drawings or specifications and were generally not following good industry practice. A regulator and an industry body therefore independently identify inadequate curing of slabs as a recurring site-practice issue.

Constructability consequence. The issue is not limited to material compliance or concrete acceptance. In suspended slabs and beams, concrete strength affects punching shear, elastic modulus, creep, shrinkage and deflection. If the in-place concrete performs below the strength assumed in design, the consequence may be a serviceability or structural concern that is difficult to resolve after construction, such as cracked finishes, excessive deflection, ponding, or punching-shear reassessment. The grade-down rule is therefore framed as an early design-stage control against later investigation, disruption, remediation cost and construction logistics problems, rather than merely as a concrete-quality issue.

What we cannot claim. The magnitude of the penalty is well supported. The frequency with which slabs are inadequately cured is not quantified in the reviewed sources: the available sources use words such as ‘often’, ‘many’ and ‘several sites’, and no empirical dataset has been found that gives a percentage of Australian slabs that are poorly cured. This entry treats inadequate or unverified curing as the realistic default for thin suspended slabs on the basis of qualitative regulator-and-industry evidence and consistent practitioner experience — a defensible working assumption, not a measured failure rate.

Limitations of the figure. The 0.77 factor is an average across pooled studies, not a prediction for a particular pour; the actual penalty depends on the mix, weather, element geometry and how much curing was actually done. Blended binders such as fly ash and slag can be more curing-sensitive than plain cement. The grade-down rule is a conservative design choice that lands on a convenient standard grade, not a precise calculation of in-place strength.

References
ACI Committee 214. (2003). Guide for Obtaining Cores and Interpreting Compressive Strength Results (ACI 214.4R-03). American Concrete Institute.
Bartlett, F. M., & MacGregor, J. G. (1996). Statistical analysis of the compressive strength of concrete in structures. ACI Materials Journal, 93(2), 158–168.
Cement Concrete & Aggregates Australia. (2020). Guide to Concrete Construction, Part V, Section 15: Curing. CCAA.
Access Canberra. (2023). Construction Note 2023/03: Concrete curing requirements. ACT Government.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by research on in-place strength reduction in poorly cured shallow elements, together with industry and regulator evidence that curing is often inadequate or unverified.

items.name: Rock sockets: founding on rock vs. a drilled socket

data.question: Where a bored pier, post or footing is shown socketed into rock, is the socket performing a defined structural or geotechnical function — or could the foundation bear on cleaned competent rock without a drilled socket?
data.parameter: Specified socket depth into rock (mm), read together with the structural or geotechnical function the socket is intended to perform.
data.background:
Rock socketing can be a legitimate foundation requirement. A socket is justified where the foundation relies on a geotechnical mechanism that bearing on cleaned rock alone cannot provide — for example uplift or tension resistance, lateral resistance, moment or fixity at the base, shaft (side) resistance for axial capacity, settlement control, or resistance to sliding.

The constructability issue is that a note such as "socket 300 mm into rock" can quietly change the job from a bearing task into a rock-drilling task, and drilling effort and cost rise steeply with depth. A shallow key is cheap; a deep socket is slow and expensive. The deeper the socket, the stronger the structural reason needs to be to justify it. A 500 mm socket included out of habit, where the structure only needs compression bearing, commits real cost for no design benefit.

This entry is not saying rock sockets should be avoided. It is saying they should be intentional. Where bearing on cleaned, inspected competent rock satisfies the design, found on rock rather than drilling a socket by default. Where the socket is required, identify the function it performs so the requirement is deliberate and can be shown clearly on the drawings. Whether the required socket can then be drilled with the plant likely to be available on the site is a separate, logistics-stage question — see the related logistics entry.

thresholds.guidance: Where compression bearing on cleaned, inspected competent rock satisfies the design, do not drill a socket. Found on rock at the nominated geotechnical bearing pressure. This assumes the structure does not rely on uplift, lateral resistance, moment fixity, side resistance or settlement control — if it does, a socket may be required and "no socket" is not appropriate.

A shallow key into rock is inexpensive and may be reasonable for minor seating, embedment, tolerance or local stability. Confirm the key is doing something useful rather than being a habitual note, but at this depth the justification burden is light.

At this depth the socket commits real drilling effort, so it should tie to an identifiable structural or geotechnical function — uplift, lateral resistance, moment/fixity, side resistance or settlement control. If the foundation only needs compression bearing, found on cleaned rock instead. If the socket is required, keep it and make the function and depth clear on the drawings.

A deep, expensive socket with no documented load-path reason should be resolved before the footing layout is fixed. Either identify the function that requires it (uplift, lateral, moment/fixity, side resistance, settlement control) and keep it as a deliberate requirement, or remove it and bear on cleaned competent rock. Do not leave a deep rock socket as an unexplained default detail. The flag marks the cost of an unjustified socket, not a prohibition on deep sockets that are genuinely required.

alternatives.title: Identify the structural function before specifying a socket
alternatives.description: Before drawing a socket, decide whether the foundation relies on uplift or tension resistance, lateral resistance, moment/fixity, shaft (side) resistance, settlement control or resistance to sliding. If the socket is not doing one of these, it is most likely a habitual detail rather than a design requirement.

alternatives.title: Found on cleaned rock where compression bearing is sufficient
alternatives.description: Where the foundation only needs vertical compression support and the geotechnical information supports an adequate bearing pressure on competent rock, found on cleaned and inspected rock rather than drilling a socket. Do not turn a bearing problem into a rock-drilling problem unless the socket is actually required.

alternatives.title: Match the socket depth to the function, not to habit
alternatives.description: Make the depth proportionate to what the socket is doing. A shallow key for seating or tolerance needs little justification; a deep socket should correspond to a real, identified load path. Avoid carrying a generous default depth across a schedule when the function does not call for it.

alternatives.title: Make the requirement and its basis clear on the drawings
alternatives.description: Where a socket is required, distinguish "found on rock" from "socket into rock", dimension the socket depth below the nominated founding rock level, and state the nominated rock class or competent rock requirement where the geotechnical design provides it. The aim is to make the required construction outcome unambiguous, not to explain the designer's reasoning.

alternatives.title: Check buildability separately once necessity is settled
alternatives.description: Deciding the socket is necessary is a Construction Methods question. Whether it can be drilled with the plant likely to be available on the site — standard auger, rock auger or specialist rig — is a separate Logistics question. Settle necessity here first, then check buildability in the companion logistics entry.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to bored piers, bored piles, post holes and small foundation excavations where the design shows penetration into rock, especially on residential and low-rise projects where a socket may be specified out of habit rather than for a defined structural function.

checks.title: Structural or geotechnical function of the socket
checks.description: The correct response depends on what the socket is doing. A socket required for uplift, lateral resistance, moment/fixity, side resistance or settlement control should not be removed for convenience. A socket included by habit where bearing on cleaned rock would suffice should be questioned.

checks.title: Sufficiency of bearing on cleaned rock
checks.description: Check whether founding on cleaned, inspected competent rock satisfies the design on its own. If compression bearing is all the structure needs, a socket adds cost without benefit.

checks.title: Rock type and strength
checks.description: Weathered or fractured sandstone is not the same problem as hard competent rock, basalt or very high-strength material. Socket depth should be considered with the geotechnical description, not only as a number on the footing schedule.

checks.title: Socket depth proportionate to function
checks.description: A shallow key and a deep socket carry very different cost. The depth should reflect the function it performs; a generous default depth repeated across a schedule is a common source of avoidable rock-drilling work.

checks.title: Geotechnical design basis
checks.description: This entry does not replace geotechnical advice. The available bearing pressure, rock classification, founding depth, socket side resistance and inspection requirements must come from the geotechnical design basis.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability review prompt. The thresholds scale the justification expected of a socket: no socket is preferred where bearing on cleaned rock is enough; a shallow key carries a light burden; deeper sockets need a clear structural or geotechnical reason.

data.evidence:
Why sockets may be required. Carter and Kulhawy (1992) analyse rock-socketed shafts under lateral loads and overturning moments, showing the socketed length below rock level providing lateral resistance and moment fixity. Asem and Gardoni (2021) address the axial response of rock sockets in weak rock, including compression and tension/uplift behaviour. These sources support the principle that a socket may be structurally necessary where the foundation does more than bear in compression.

Why a socket should be a deliberate decision, not a default note. Haberfield and Collingwood (2006) show that rock-socketed pile performance is construction-sensitive, depending on rock type, strength, socket roughness, smear, drilling-fluid residue, cleanliness and drilling technique. A socket is therefore a considered design and construction requirement, not a habitual addition to a footing schedule.

Buildability basis. Wong et al. (2006) identify economical use of contractor plant, labour and resources as a leading buildability factor. Applied to foundations, an unnecessary rock socket commits non-standard drilling effort and cost for no design benefit — the avoidable case this entry targets.

Threshold basis. The 150 mm and 300 mm hinges are design-stage constructability prompts based on practitioner judgement, not published design limits. They scale the justification expected of a socket: a shallow key needs little reason, a deeper socket needs a clear structural or geotechnical function. The depth thresholds should be refined over time using project examples and regional feedback.

References
Carter, J. P., & Kulhawy, F. H. (1992). Analysis of laterally loaded shafts in rock. Journal of Geotechnical Engineering, 118(6), 839-855.
Asem, P., & Gardoni, P. (2021). On the use and interpretation of in situ load tests in weak rock masses. Rock Mechanics and Rock Engineering, 54, 3663-3700.
Haberfield, C., & Collingwood, B. (2006). Rock-socketed pile design and construction: a better way? Proceedings of the Institution of Civil Engineers - Geotechnical Engineering, 159(GE3), 207-217.
Wong, F. W. H., Lam, P. T. I., Chan, E. H. W., & Wong, F. K. W. (2006). Factors affecting buildability of building designs. Canadian Journal of Civil Engineering, 33, 795-806.

supportingImages.title:
Why a socket may be required: lateral load and moment
The socketed length below rock level provides lateral resistance and moment fixity that cannot be achieved by bearing on cleaned rock. A socket doing this work is a deliberate design requirement, not a habitual note. Source: Carter & Kulhawy (1992).

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by peer-reviewed rock-socket design literature establishing the structural functions that justify a socket (Carter & Kulhawy; Asem & Gardoni), construction-sensitivity findings (Haberfield & Collingwood) and buildability research (Wong et al.). The 150 mm and 300 mm depth hinges are practitioner judgement rather than a published design rule and should be refined with project examples.

relatedEntries.note: Companion logistics entry. Once the socket is confirmed as necessary here, that entry covers whether it can be drilled with the plant likely to be available on the site.
Both concern foundation system selection where ground conditions and required embedment change the practical construction method.
Both concern the constructability consequences of the chosen pier or pile detailing method.

items.name: Pour sequencing for deep vertical concrete elements at slab interfaces (example: walls/columns/piles)

data.question: Where a slab, beam, ground beam or pile cap is cast against or above a deep vertical concrete element, has the design made the intended pour sequence clear and considered plastic settlement, construction-joint and water-ingress risks?
data.parameter: Depth difference between the slab or horizontal member and the deeper vertical concrete element at the interface (mm).
data.background:
Fresh concrete continues to settle while it is still plastic. In shallow or uniform slab sections this movement is usually small, but it can become significant where a much deeper vertical concrete section is cast against a thinner slab or horizontal member.

This entry is not intended to suggest that normal monolithic beam-and-slab construction is poor practice. Integral beams, band beams, drop panels, slab thickenings and drop edge beams are commonly poured with slabs. The issue is whether the design recognises the scale of differential plastic settlement where the vertical depth becomes large.

For full-height walls, columns and deep piers or piles, the usual constructability response is to pour the vertical element separately, stop it at the intended joint or soffit, and show starter bars, laps, couplers or continuity reinforcement as required.

For deep drop edge beams, downstand beams or slab-on-ground edge conditions, a separate pour may not always be the best response. A construction joint near ground level, external exposure or a retaining condition can introduce water ingress risk. In those cases, a controlled monolithic pour may be preferable, provided settlement, compaction, vibration, possible revibration and reinforcement congestion are considered.

Additional reinforcement is not a substitute for pour sequence. Trimmer bars at a re-entrant corner help control hardened-concrete tensile cracking. Plastic settlement occurs while the concrete is still fresh, before the reinforcement is acting in the normal hardened-concrete way. Reinforcement can also restrain settlement and contribute to cracking or bleed-water pockets around bars.

thresholds.guidance: The intended pour sequence is clear. For walls, columns, piers and piles, this normally means the vertical element is poured separately to the intended joint or soffit, with starter bars, laps, couplers or continuity reinforcement shown as required. For deep drop edge beams, downstand beams, slab thickenings, pile caps or ground beams, this may mean a monolithic pour is deliberately retained, with the construction method able to manage settlement, compaction and water ingress risk.

A deep vertical concrete element is shown monolithic with a much shallower slab or horizontal member, but the pour strategy is unclear. This is especially relevant where a full-height wall, column, pier or pile appears to be poured at the same time as the slab, beam, ground beam or pile cap above; or where a deep drop edge beam or downstand beam is shown without recognition of plastic settlement, compaction, revibration or water-ingress implications.

workedExample.title: Worked example — indicative differential plastic movement
workedExample.caveat: The table applies an indicative plastic settlement range of 0.005–0.02 mm/mm to common concrete depths. It assumes a 150 mm slab and estimates the differential movement between the slab and the deeper vertical section. Values are indicative only and show scale, not exact site movement.
workedExample.headline: A few millimetres at a slab thickening can become tens of millimetres at a wall, column, pile or deep edge condition.
tableHeaders: Indicative differential plastic movement compared with 150 mm slab
tableRows: Minor thickening. Usually handled by normal placement and vibration.
Deep beam or local thickening. Settlement at the transition should be considered.
Large beam, edge beam or pier head. Monolithic pour may still be suitable, but settlement control matters.
Large drop edge beam. Do not assume it behaves like a normal slab edge; balance settlement risk against water-ingress risk from any joint.
Storey-height wall, column or deep vertical element. Separate vertical pour should normally be expected.
Typical wall or column height. Same-time pouring with the slab above should require a deliberate reviewed method.
High wall/column lift or deep vertical element. Show a separate pour unless a specific construction method is agreed.
workedExample.howToUse: Compare the depth of the deeper concrete section with the slab or horizontal member it is being cast against. Use the estimated differential movement to decide whether the design should show a separate pour, or whether a deliberate monolithic pour with settlement control is the better construction method. The table is a constructability prompt, not a structural deflection calculation.
assumptions: Published settlement range used: 0.005–0.02 mm/mm of concrete depth, reported for concrete slab depths in the approximate 300–1000 mm range.
Indicative differential movement = (deeper concrete section depth − 150 mm slab depth) × 0.005 to 0.02.
Values above 1000 mm extrapolate the published range to show the scale of the mechanism at common wall, column and deep edge-beam heights; they are not predictions of exact movement on site.
workedExample.assumptionsNote: Actual behaviour depends on mix design, slump, bleeding, placement rate, vibration, reinforcement congestion, formwork geometry, temperature and timing. The worked example shows why depth difference matters; it does not create a code limit.

alternatives.title: Pour full-height vertical elements separately from the slab where practical
alternatives.description: For full-height vertical elements, such as walls and columns above approximately 2.4 m, the preferred constructability approach is usually to pour the vertical element separately from the slab, beam, ground beam or pile cap above. Stop the wall or column at the intended construction joint or soffit, allow the concrete to settle and leave the plastic state, and connect the next pour using starter bars, laps, couplers or other continuity reinforcement as required.

alternatives.title: Treat deep piers and piles the same way where they connect into caps, ground beams or slabs
alternatives.description: For bored piers, piles or similar deep vertical elements, avoid implying that a deep vertical pour and the slab, pile cap or ground beam above are placed as one continuous fresh-concrete pour. Where practical, pour the pier or pile first and connect into the member above with starters or continuity reinforcement.

alternatives.title: Do not automatically split deep edge beams or slab thickenings
alternatives.description: Deep drop edge beams, downstand beams and slab thickenings may still be best poured monolithically with the slab, especially near ground level, exposed edges, retaining conditions or wet areas where a construction joint could create a water ingress problem. In these cases, the recommendation is not “add a joint”; it is to deliberately manage the monolithic pour through placement sequence, vibration, possible revibration, mix control and reinforcement congestion review.

alternatives.title: Detail water control where a joint is introduced near exposure
alternatives.description: Where a construction joint is introduced near ground level, exposed edges, retaining conditions or wet areas, detail the water-control strategy deliberately. This may require a waterstop, joint preparation, waterproofing continuity, sealant, surface preparation or another project-specific treatment.

alternatives.title: Do not rely on extra reinforcement alone to solve plastic settlement
alternatives.description: Additional reinforcement may help control hardened-concrete cracking, but it does not prevent fresh concrete from settling or bleeding while plastic. In the plastic state, reinforcement can also act as a restraint and contribute to cracking or bleed-water pockets around bars. The primary control is the pour strategy, not simply adding more steel.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to deep vertical concrete elements at slab, beam, ground beam and pile cap interfaces. Examples include walls, columns, bored piers, piles, deep drop edge beams, downstand beams and other deep vertical legs cast against a thinner horizontal member.

checks.title: This is an interface issue, not a rule for all vertical pours
checks.description: A lift shaft wall or core wall poured several metres high is a different constructability issue unless it is being combined with a slab, beam or cap at the same interface. That case is mainly about formwork pressure, rate of rise, compaction access, cold joints and concrete placement procedure.

checks.title: Normal beam-and-slab construction is not the target
checks.description: Integral beams, band beams, drop panels and slab thickenings are commonly poured monolithically with slabs. This entry targets deep-to-shallow geometry where the vertical settlement potential is large enough to influence the pour method.

checks.title: Drop edge and downstand beams need judgement
checks.description: A deep drop edge beam may have meaningful plastic settlement potential, but a separate construction joint near ground or external exposure can create a water-ingress problem. The better constructability response may be a controlled monolithic pour rather than a joint.

checks.title: Piers and piles are included where they interface with a cap, beam or slab
checks.description: Deep bored piers or piles are normally poured separately with starters into the cap, beam or slab above. Where a detail implies otherwise, the same settlement and interface logic should be reviewed.

checks.title: The worked example is not a code limit
checks.description: The table uses an indicative settlement range to show order of magnitude. It should not be used as a structural deflection calculation, concrete acceptance criterion or mandatory cutoff.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability prompt. The aim is not to ban monolithic pouring, but to make the pour strategy deliberate where deep vertical concrete is cast against a much shallower slab, beam, ground beam or pile cap.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by research on plastic settlement and plastic cracking in fresh concrete. The evidence supports the mechanism and the order of magnitude used in the worked example, but it does not provide a hard code-style cutoff for when a pour must be separated.

Mechanism — differential plastic settlement. Kayondo, Combrinck and Boshoff (2019) review plastic cracking research and identify plastic settlement cracking as a differential-settlement problem caused by restraints such as changes in section depth and reinforcement. Their T-beam figure is useful because it illustrates cracking at a deep-to-shallow section change. They also state that greater differences in vertical volume change between settlement zones increase cracking potential, and identify concrete depth as a main factor influencing settlement.

Settlement range and reinforcement effect. Kwak, Ha and Weiss (2010) experimentally and numerically quantified plastic settlement in fresh cementitious systems. Their work supports self-weight settlement as a real mechanism and reports settlement values that can be used to estimate the order of magnitude of potential movement. They also show that reinforcement can restrain settlement, with water pockets forming below bars and tensile stress developing above bars. This is why additional reinforcement is not a substitute for sequencing or settlement control.

How the worked example is used. The worked example applies the published settlement range of 0.005–0.02 mm/mm to common concrete depths and compares them with a 150 mm slab. Values above 1000 mm are extrapolated to show the scale of the mechanism at wall, column, pile and deep edge-beam depths. The table is therefore a constructability prompt, not a prediction of exact site movement.

Constructability interpretation. For full-height walls, columns, piers and piles, the usual practical response is to pour the vertical element separately and show starter/continuity reinforcement. For deep drop edge beams and similar edge conditions, a monolithic pour may still be the better response because a construction joint can create water-ingress risk. The design should make that choice deliberate rather than accidental.

References
Kayondo, M., Combrinck, R., & Boshoff, W. P. (2019). State-of-the-art review on plastic cracking of concrete. Construction and Building Materials, 225, 886–899. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.conbuildmat.2019.07.197
Kwak, H.-G., Ha, S., & Weiss, W. J. (2010). Experimental and numerical quantification of plastic settlement in fresh cementitious systems. Journal of Materials in Civil Engineering, 22(10), 951–966. https://doi.org/10.1061/(ASCE)MT.1943-5533.0000097

supportingImages.title:
Plastic settlement at a deep-to-shallow concrete section — Kayondo et al. (2019), Fig. 4(b,d)

The deeper section settles more than the adjacent shallow slab while the concrete is still plastic, creating differential movement and cracking at the interface. This T-beam example from Kayondo, Combrinck and Boshoff, State-of-the-art review on plastic cracking of concrete, illustrates the mechanism behind this entry: as the vertical depth below a slab interface increases, plastic settlement can become large enough to affect the pour strategy. It is not intended to suggest that ordinary beam-and-slab pours are unacceptable.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by plastic-settlement and plastic-cracking research, including an order-of-magnitude settlement range and a clear differential-settlement mechanism. The worked example extrapolates beyond the tested depth range and is a constructability prompt rather than a hard rule, so the support level is medium.

relatedEntries.note: Both concern how the concrete is staged and poured in sequence so trades keep flowing and joints and settlement are controlled.

items.name: Concrete columns/walls vs a blockwork-and-steel combination

data.question: For vertical members supporting a suspended concrete floor, are reinforced concrete columns or walls the more constructable choice — or would a blockwork-and-steel combination build more simply?
data.parameter: The chosen vertical support system (concrete columns/walls vs a blockwork-and-steel combination), assessed against how much the floors repeat and whether the member has a concrete-specific structural or fire role.
data.background:
A suspended concrete floor does not force concrete supports beneath it. The same deck can sit on reinforced concrete columns and walls, on blockwork walls, or on steel posts — and very often a mix. The constructability question is not whether concrete can do the job, but which support system builds more simply for this particular structure. Concrete is the obvious answer in some cases and an expensive habit in others, and the difference is usually predictable.

Two things mostly decide it. The first is repetition. Reinforced concrete is a wet-trade cycle — form, fix, pour, cure, strike — in which formwork, not concrete, dominates the labour. Where a building has many similar floors, that formwork cycles and the crew climbs a learning curve, so concrete columns and walls become very efficient and are almost always the right call; the trade is already mobilised and the marginal member is cheap. Where floors are few, irregular or non-repeating, the reuse never develops and a one-off member carries the full fixed overhead of a pour event (a pour day, a pump or crane visit, joint preparation, an inspection hold) for very little work. The second is whether the member has a concrete-specific role that a blockwork-and-steel scheme cannot easily replace: part of the lateral system (a core or shear wall), a column under a flat plate where the monolithic head carries punching shear and moment continuity, inherent fire resistance, an aggressive durability exposure, or an off-form architectural finish. Where one of these governs, concrete earns its place regardless of repetition.

Where neither applies — an isolated, gravity-only vertical member on a low-rise, non-repetitive job, with the rest of the support already in blockwork or steel — a blockwork wall (which needs no formwork, stripping or curing) or a steel post (erected dry, fast and out of sequence) will often build more simply and let the deck land on a support that is already up. A concrete-filled steel hollow section is a hybrid that keeps concrete capacity or appearance while avoiding the wet forming cycle.

This entry deliberately does not declare which option is cheaper. Published frame-comparison studies disagree, and the result is sensitive to region, market, scale and the project's fire and lateral strategy. The entry's job is to flag when the comparison is worth running and which way the constructability factors lean — not to issue a cost verdict.

thresholds.guidance: Either the building has many similar, repeating floors so the formwork cycles and the concrete trade is already mobilised, or the member has a concrete-specific role a blockwork-and-steel scheme cannot easily replace (a lateral core or shear wall, a flat-plate column head carrying punching and continuity, inherent fire resistance, aggressive durability, or off-form architecture). Concrete columns or walls are the more constructable choice and drawing them in concrete is justified, not habitual.

Some repetition exists but floors vary, or it is a genuinely close mid-rise call, and the member has no clear concrete-specific role. Concrete is plausibly fine, but confirm the formwork reuse actually develops across the floors as drawn rather than being fragmented by varied geometry. Where the call is marginal, develop the blockwork-and-steel option in parallel, and if it remains unclear, ask a builder or contractor in your network which would build more easily before committing.

A single suspended level or a low-rise, non-repetitive job, where isolated vertical members would each trigger their own pour overhead and none has a concrete-specific structural or fire role. Casting them in concrete means mobilising a vertical concrete operation largely for these members. Carry a blockwork-and-steel scheme as a real option and, if the easier-to-build choice is not obvious, seek a builder or contractor's view before the structural scheme is fixed; the alternative will often build more simply.

Isolated concrete columns or walls on an otherwise dry, low-rise, non-repetitive structure — with the rest of the support already in blockwork or steel and no lateral, flat-plate, fire or durability reason for concrete. The full wet-trade cycle is being committed for no design benefit while simpler trades are already on site. Resolve the material choice before the frame and programme lock: either confirm a reason concrete is required, or switch to blockwork and/or steel coordinated with the existing work. The flag marks an unexamined default, not a prohibition on concrete where it is the right answer.

alternatives.title: Establish floor repetition first
alternatives.description: Decide how much the floors actually repeat. Many similar floors let formwork cycle and the crew climb the learning curve, which makes concrete columns and walls efficient and usually the right call. Few, irregular or non-repeating floors never develop that reuse, and an isolated member carries the full fixed overhead of its own pour event — the case where a blockwork-and-steel alternative is most worth weighing against concrete.

alternatives.title: Identify any concrete-specific role
alternatives.description: Confirm whether the member has a role that blockwork and steel cannot easily replace: part of the lateral system (a core or shear wall), a column under a flat plate where the monolithic head carries punching shear and moment continuity, inherent fire resistance, an aggressive durability exposure, or an off-form architectural finish. If one of these governs, concrete earns its place regardless of repetition. Note that pinning a steel column under a flat plate changes the slab design.

alternatives.title: Consider blockwork and steel as a scheme
alternatives.description: A suspended concrete deck can sit on blockwork walls and steel posts. Blockwork needs no formwork, stripping or curing and can go up ahead of the deck; steel posts erect dry, fast and out of sequence; and the deck lands on a support that is already up rather than one that must be cast, cured and stripped first. Treat this as a genuine scheme option, not a fallback.

alternatives.title: Consider a concrete-filled hollow section
alternatives.description: Where a concrete look, axial capacity or fire performance is wanted but the wet forming cycle is the problem, a concrete-filled CHS or SHS uses the permanent tube as both formwork and reinforcement, avoiding conventional stripping. It can be erected dry and filled later, capturing part of the steel programme benefit while retaining concrete capacity.

alternatives.title: Ask a builder where it is marginal
alternatives.description: Where the constructability lean is not clear, which system is simpler and cheaper to build turns on regional, market and trade factors a design engineer cannot settle from the drawings. Rather than attempting to estimate it, ask a builder or contractor in your network for a practical opinion on which option would build more easily in the local market. Their feedback at concept stage is quick to obtain and usually decides a marginal call.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to vertical members supporting a suspended concrete structure where both reinforced concrete (columns or walls) and a blockwork-and-steel combination are structurally viable. It is most useful on low-rise and mixed-system buildings where the support material is genuinely open. It is not intended for buildings with many repeating floors where formwork cycles efficiently in concrete, nor where a lateral, flat-plate, fire, durability or architectural requirement clearly settles the choice in favour of concrete.

checks.title: Floor repetition and similarity
checks.description: Many similar, repeating floors let formwork cycle and the crew climb a learning curve, which favours concrete. Few, irregular or non-repeating floors fragment the reuse and weaken the concrete case. Judge the repetition the design actually delivers, not the nominal storey count.

checks.title: Whether the concrete trade is already mobilised
checks.description: If the structure has a concrete core, walls or repeating decks, a concrete column rides along on a trade, formwork system, pump and crew already on site, so its marginal cost is small. If casting these members would mobilise a vertical concrete operation largely for them, the overhead is far harder to justify.

checks.title: Concrete-specific structural role
checks.description: Check whether the member is part of the lateral system or sits under a flat plate where the monolithic head carries punching and continuity. These are structural reasons to keep concrete; substituting steel or blockwork can change the slab design or the lateral system, not just the member.

checks.title: Fire, durability and finish
checks.description: Inherent fire resistance, aggressive exposure, or a required off-form finish can tie the member to concrete. A steel alternative typically needs applied fire and corrosion protection with its own cost, inspection and maintenance; weigh these in the comparison.

checks.title: Connection and sequence
checks.description: Steel posts and blockwork walls change the build sequence (support up first, deck lands on top) and require connection details to the deck, footings and any concrete elements. The base plate, holding-down and bearing details are part of the comparison, not an afterthought.

checks.title: Cost is regional and out of scope
checks.description: This entry does not decide which option is cheaper. Published frame-comparison studies disagree and the result depends on region, market, scale and fire/lateral strategy. Use the entry to decide when a builder's or contractor's practical view is worth seeking; obtaining a price is not a design-engineer task.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability review prompt, not a rule that isolated members must be steel or blockwork. The bands scale how strongly the structure points toward a cycling concrete trade or a defined concrete role: repetition of similar floors or a lateral/flat-plate/fire role favours concrete; an isolated member on a low-rise, non-repetitive job with no such role is where a blockwork-and-steel scheme is most worth weighing. Structural adequacy, fire, durability and connection design of any alternative remain the engineer's responsibility under the relevant standards.

data.evidence:
Why repetition favours concrete. Reinforced concrete is a wet-trade cycle in which formwork, not concrete, dominates the labour: Löfgren et al. (2001) attribute roughly 49% of concrete-structure work to formwork against about 11% to concrete operations, and identify simplicity and repetition as key efficiency principles. Because formwork is the cost, reusing it is what makes concrete economical. Jarkas (2010) measured this directly, finding that repetition of floor layout and standardisation significantly improve formwork labour productivity on building floors. The mechanism is a learning-curve effect — as the same floor layout and the same formwork are repeated, unit labour time falls and output climbs — which is what those productivity gains reflect. Many similar floors therefore make concrete columns and walls efficient; few or irregular floors do not.

Why a dry or simpler trade can win otherwise. The foundational buildability principles (CIRIA, 1983; Adams, 1989) call both for maximum repetition and standardisation and for modular and pre-assembly designs that facilitate prefabrication and installation. The first favours repeated in-situ concrete; the second favours a steel post erected dry and out of sequence, or a blockwork wall that needs no formwork, stripping or curing and can be built ahead of the deck. Which principle dominates depends on the structure, which is why this is a genuine trade-off rather than a fixed preference.

Why concrete sometimes earns its place regardless of repetition. A vertical member that forms part of the lateral system (a core or shear wall), or a column under a flat plate where the monolithic head carries punching shear and moment continuity, performs a structural function that substituting steel or blockwork would change, not merely relocate. Fire resistance and durability can do the same. These are design requirements governed by the relevant structural and fire standards (in Australia, AS 3600 and AS 4100) and remain the engineer's responsibility; the entry prompts the comparison, it does not pre-empt that design.

Why the entry does not name a cost winner. Published frame-comparison studies reach opposing conclusions that track the interest publishing them: steel-industry cost studies report steel frames modestly cheaper and faster to erect, while concrete- and masonry-industry studies report concrete or concrete-masonry systems cheaper, and independent academic comparisons (typically software cost models) contradict one another and are highly sensitive to their assumptions. The one robust, cross-source agreement is that steel erects faster. Because the cost result depends on region, market, scale and the project's fire and lateral strategy, this entry flags when a builder's or contractor's practical input is worth seeking rather than declaring an outcome.

Basis of the bands. The bands scale how strongly the structure points toward a cycling concrete trade or a defined concrete role. The repetition leg is supported by measured formwork-productivity research; the structural-role gate by standard design behaviour; the cost dimension is deliberately left to a builder or contractor's input. The exact tipping point in the middle (the close mid-rise call) is practitioner judgement and should be refined with project examples and regional feedback.

References
Adams, S. (1989). Practical Buildability (CIRIA Building Design Report). Butterworths, London.
CIRIA (1983). Buildability: An Assessment. CIRIA Special Publication 26, London.
Jarkas, A. M. (2010). Buildability factors affecting formwork labour productivity of building floors. Canadian Journal of Civil Engineering, 37(10), 1383-1394.
Löfgren, I., Gylltoft, K., & Kutti, T. (2001). In-situ concrete building - Innovations in Formwork. Department of Structural Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology / Thomas Concrete Group.
Wong, F. W. H., Lam, P. T. I., Chan, E. H. W., & Wong, F. K. W. (2006). Factors affecting buildability of building designs. Canadian Journal of Civil Engineering, 33, 795-806.

evidenceSupport.summary: The core lean is well supported: measured formwork-productivity research shows repetition and standardisation drive concrete efficiency (Jarkas, 2010; Löfgren et al., 2001), reinforced by the learning-curve effect that repeated, similar work produces and by the foundational buildability principles (CIRIA, 1983; Adams, 1989), which also back the prefabrication advantage of steel. The concrete-specific role gate (lateral systems, flat-plate column heads, fire) rests on standard design behaviour and the relevant standards. The entry deliberately does not adjudicate the cost comparison, which published frame studies dispute and which is region-dependent, and the mid-rise tipping point between bands is practitioner judgement. Treat as a constructability prompt; the exact band boundaries should be refined with project examples.

relatedEntries.note: The repetition leg of this entry in detail: how formwork cycling on repeated floors drives the economy of in-situ concrete framing.
Both concern the cost of concrete pour cycles; this entry can remove the cycle by changing material, that one reschedules it to protect the programme.
Companion material/system-choice question where a dry, fabricated alternative can remove a wet or plant-heavy in-situ process.

items.name: Bored concrete piles vs. screw piles

data.question: On a lightly-to-moderately loaded foundation, has the program and site-disruption advantage of screw piles been weighed against bored concrete piles — or has the pile type been chosen on capacity alone, without considering same-day loading, no spoil, and no curing wait?
data.parameter: Foundation loading and site constraints, used to weigh screw piles (fast, no spoil, no curing wait, excavator-installable) against bored concrete piles (higher capacity, spoil + curing).
data._evidenceAngle: Programme/buildability, like the PT entry. Direction strongly supported by industry sources: screw piles install in hours, no curing delay (same-day load), no spoil to cart, and can be installed with an excavator rather than a dedicated rig; bored piers favoured for heavy loads / deep sand. For the framework standard, anchor authority to a geotechnical reference (Perko, Helical Piles: A Practical Guide to Design and Installation, 2009) and use installer sources only to corroborate direction (as the PT entry uses supplier literature). Keep the honest counter-case so it stays a 'weigh this' entry. Links to the Equipment access entry.
data._thresholdSketch: Good/Consider by loading + site: light loads + tight/sensitive access → weigh screw piles strongly. Heavy loads / deep sand → bored may govern. Frame as weigh-the-program-advantage, not a rule.

relatedEntries.note: Both are early system-selection decisions where the constructability case — not just structural capacity — should drive the choice. Weigh them together when settling the scheme.
Both are early system-selection decisions where the constructability case — not just structural capacity — should drive the choice. Weigh them together when settling the scheme.
Both depend on whether construction plant and workers can physically reach and work the element on this site — check site access together.

items.name: Stay-in-place formwork and masonry wall systems

data.question: Has the wall system (conventional formed RC, stay-in-place / permanent formwork, or reinforced masonry) been chosen to suit how the wall can actually be built on this site — above all whether both faces can be accessed and stripped — rather than defaulting to two-sided formed RC where that is not practical?
data.parameter: Wall system selection against site buildability: chiefly access for stripping (both faces accessible vs single-sided / restricted), element repetition, and the required surface finish.
data._evidenceAngle: SELECTION entry (when to use each), not a generic 'fewer trades' claim. The sharpest, best-supported pivot is ACCESS: where the back face cannot be reached to strip a form — blindside basement walls, walls tight to a boundary or existing structure, retaining walls against an excavation face, and repetitive linear edge runs such as crash barriers / parapets — permanent / single-sided / stay-in-place formwork moves from 'an alternative' to clearly preferable, because two-sided formed RC physically cannot be stripped there. Practice sources are consistent: permanent formwork is best where access is limited and stripping is impractical; single-sided formwork is specifically for foundation/retaining walls where a second form cannot be deployed; blindside systems form walls within inches of an existing structure. Conventional formed RC stays preferable for high off-form finish and intricate/complex shapes (SIP has shape/structural limitations). Reinforced masonry (blockwork) suits low-rise boundary/retaining walls where a masonry trade is economical and no form hire is wanted. CRASH BARRIERS: the instinct holds on the logic (repetitive linear run + awkward edge access + no strip) — treat as an example to confirm with a barrier-specific source, not yet a sourced claim. CAUTIONS to bake in: higher upfront cost; accredited-installer warranties; polymer faces force a rethink of waterproofing/coatings/cladding; tall unfilled PVC walls behave like a sail in wind (a real erection hazard). Keep GENERIC — do not endorse proprietary products. Source leads: Architecture & Design (AU) permanent-formwork pros/cons; one-sided/blindside formwork suppliers for the access logic; seek an independent productivity/selection comparison to lift above supplier claims.
data._thresholdSketch: Best practice: system matched to access + finish (single-sided/permanent where the back face cannot be stripped; formed RC where two-sided access and a high finish are needed). Consider: either could work — weigh repetition, finish, trade availability, upfront cost. Concern: two-sided formed RC specified where back-face access is genuinely restricted. Action required: a wall that cannot be formed and stripped two-sided as drawn, with no permanent/single-sided system considered.

relatedEntries.note: Both are early system-selection decisions where the constructability case — not just structural capacity — should drive the choice. Weigh them together when settling the scheme.

items.name: Void-former slab vs. thick raft

data.question: Where a thick raft is proposed, has a void-former (biaxial) suspended slab on piles been weighed against it — considering not just concrete volume but the buildability of a large, thick pour and its heat-of-hydration cracking risk?
data.parameter: Foundation/slab system where a thick raft is proposed, assessed against a void-former slab for pour buildability, self-weight and early thermal cracking.
data._evidenceAngle: Strongest tie is the mass-concrete THERMAL angle (more rigorous than 'uses less concrete'): a thick raft is a large heat-of-hydration pour prone to early thermal cracking, hard to build well; a biaxial void slab sidesteps the thick-pour problem and cuts self-weight. Practice reference: CIRIA C660 (controlling early-age thermal cracking) as a construction-practice guide, NOT a code clause. Keep the AS mass-concrete provisions in the back of mind only.
data._thresholdSketch: Good/Consider by pour thickness + thermal risk: thin/normal → fine. Concern: thick raft with real thermal-cracking control burden. Action required: very thick pour where a void slab would clearly ease buildability — weigh it.

dimensions.name: Detailing
dimensions.description: Standardisation, repetition, and simplification of element detailing
generalGuidance: Prefer consistent reinforcement layouts across top and bottom steel where structurally appropriate - this simplifies ordering, reduces placement errors, and supports safety
Simplify formwork geometry wherever possible - regular layouts and repetition improve productivity and reduce cost
Evaluate whether complex geometry is structurally necessary - simpler details that achieve comparable performance are easier to build and less prone to error

items.name: Suspended slab level changes (folds)

data.question: When a design requires a change in suspended slab levels, is monolithic forming or an alternative method (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) the more constructable approach?
data.parameter: Fold depth in suspended slab (mm)
data.background: A change in suspended slab level can be achieved by forming the slab as a monolithic profile (a continuous concrete fold) or by separating the levels using blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours. Before sizing a fold at all, the designer should consider whether the level change can be eliminated entirely by thickening the slab to a flat soffit; for small setdowns (e.g. ≤ 50 mm) this is often the simpler construction outcome. Where a fold is genuinely required, the default constructability-aware approach is generally to use an alternative method rather than monolithic forming, because alternatives use standard trades, standard formwork and conventional construction sequences, while a monolithic fold requires custom edge formwork, additional bracing and propping, and a forming approach that is not part of normal slab construction. The amount of labour required to form a vertical face increases with the depth of that face (Jarkas, 2010), so a monolithic fold becomes progressively more difficult and labour-intensive as depth increases. The thresholds below indicate how strongly alternatives should be preferred at different fold depths, based on formworker feedback about where monolithic forming becomes problematic. Waterproofing is the most common objection to separate pours, but this can generally be managed using a waterstop and standard cold joint detailing and is not usually a reason to abandon the alternative approach.

thresholds.guidance: Monolithic forming can generally be accommodated by standard formwork systems at this depth. Consistent with 3-5 stair risers within a structure. Alternatives (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) are still usually the simpler approach because they use standard trades and standard formwork, but monolithic forming remains a reasonable option at this depth where there is a specific reason for it (e.g. architectural finish, watertight element, or a short isolated fold).

Monolithic forming may be achievable but formwork complexity, bracing and labour increase substantially within this range. Alternatives should be strongly preferred unless there is a specific reason for monolithic forming. Where monolithic forming is retained, the fold length, pour volume and formwork access should be reviewed with the contractor before construction.

Monolithic forming is likely impractical using standard formwork systems. A real project example required a 1050 mm fold in a suspended RC slab. During construction, the detail was found to be impractical due to the difficulty and cost of forming with adequate bracing. The design was revised to incorporate blockwork and separate concrete pours. Alternatives should be adopted at this depth unless a specific project condition makes monolithic forming the only viable option, and even then the construction approach should be confirmed with the contractor at the design stage.

alternatives.title: Use blockwork infill with separate concrete pours
alternatives.description: Form the level change using blockwork or masonry infill, then pour the concrete elements separately using conventional formwork. This is generally the default constructability-aware approach because it uses standard trades and standard formwork rather than custom edge formwork and bracing. Where the joint needs to be watertight, this can be detailed using a waterstop and appropriate cold joint treatment. Check structural load transfer, jointing, durability and finish requirements before adopting this option. Document this alongside the monolithic detail on the drawing, not as a replacement for it — the contractor selects the method on site based on available trades and formwork, and notifies the engineer prior to construction. Both details should be fully detailed (reinforcement, cold joint treatment, waterstop where required) so the structural design covers whichever path is built.

alternatives.title: Use permanent formwork (e.g. Dincel) with separate pours
alternatives.description: Use a permanent formwork system (such as Dincel or similar) to form the vertical element of the level change, then pour the slabs separately using conventional formwork. This can be particularly useful where access for stripping is limited or where a stable, self-supporting vertical formwork is preferred. The system should be checked for structural suitability, fire and durability requirements, finish expectations and compatibility with the surrounding concrete detail. Document this alongside the monolithic detail on the drawing, not as a replacement for it — the contractor selects the method on site based on available trades and formwork, and notifies the engineer prior to construction. Both details should be fully detailed (reinforcement, cold joint treatment, waterstop where required) so the structural design covers whichever path is built.

alternatives.title: Redesign to reduce or eliminate the level change
alternatives.description: Review whether the fold depth can be reduced, relocated or removed while still satisfying the architectural, structural and serviceability requirements. Simplifying the slab profile is often the most effective constructability improvement because it reduces custom formwork, reinforcement complexity, sequencing issues and construction risk at the source.

alternatives.title: Retain monolithic forming only where specifically justified
alternatives.description: Monolithic forming may still be appropriate where the fold is short and isolated, where a continuous unbroken concrete face is required for architectural reasons, where the element must be watertight and a joint introduces unacceptable risk, or where the structural condition is one in which a cold joint is not appropriate. Where monolithic forming is retained, confirm the construction method with the contractor at the design stage rather than assuming it can be built.

data.evidence:
This entry is supported by formwork literature, industry consultation and practical project experience.

Formwork literature. Jarkas (2010) measures labour productivity for edge formwork as a function of vertical face depth, confirming that the labour required to form a vertical face increases with depth.

Industry consultation. On-site consultation with formwork subcontractors and a building contractor identified monolithic folds above approximately 600 mm as becoming progressively more difficult to form using standard panel systems, with blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours the more common construction outcome.

Practical project experience. Two project examples support the entry. A 1050 mm fold in a suspended RC slab was found impractical to form monolithically during construction and was revised to blockwork with separate pours. A drop edge beam — a vertical concrete element subject to the same forming choice — documented both monolithic and blockwork alternatives on the same drawing. The contractor used both on site across different sections of the element. This demonstrates that documenting both methods is best practice — it allowed the contractor to use whichever suited each section.

References
Jarkas, A. M. (2010). Analysis and measurement of buildability factors affecting edge formwork labour productivity. Journal of Engineering Science and Technology Review, 3(1), 142–150.

examples.caption: Original detail: 1050 mm monolithic fold in suspended RC slab. Found to be impractical during construction due to difficulty and cost of forming with adequate bracing using conventional formwork.
Revised detail: blockwork infill with separate concrete pours. Achieves the required change in slab level using standard formwork and conventional construction methods.
Drop edge beam with monolithic (left) and blockwork (right) shown on the same drawing. Contractor used both on site.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to suspended slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements where a vertical level change is being designed, whether to be formed monolithically as a continuous concrete profile or by alternative methods such as blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours.

checks.title: Consider eliminating the fold before sizing it
checks.description: The simplest constructability outcome is often no fold at all. For small setdowns (e.g. ≤ 50 mm) it is generally easier and cheaper to thicken the slab on the higher side and keep a flat soffit than to introduce a fold and form a vertical face. The extra concrete volume is usually small relative to the construction cost of forming, bracing and finishing a fold, and a flat soffit removes the constructability question entirely. For larger setdowns the trade-off shifts as the extra concrete volume becomes more significant and a separated construction approach (blockwork or permanent formwork with separate pours) is usually more appropriate. The designer needs to make a call based on the area, depth and project context; the point is that eliminating the level change should be considered before choosing how to form it, not after.

checks.title: Waterproofing is not usually a deal-breaker
checks.description: The most common objection to using separate pours is waterproofing of the cold joint between the two pours. This can generally be managed using a waterstop (PVC, hydrophilic or similar) and appropriate cold joint detailing, and is not usually a reason to retain monolithic forming. Waterproofing should be detailed and specified as part of the alternative, not used as a default justification for monolithic forming.

checks.title: Fold length and pour volume
checks.description: A short local fold is not the same construction problem as a long continuous fold. A deep fold over a few lineal metres may still be manageable monolithically, while the same depth over a long length is much more likely to require an alternative. The construction effort scales with the area of vertical formwork, not just the depth.

checks.title: Function of the level change
checks.description: The appropriate response depends on why the level change exists. Architectural set-downs, structural steps, waterproofing zones, fire or acoustic separation, retained fill and support for other elements may each require different detailing. In some cases a continuous unbroken concrete face is required for architectural or watertightness reasons, and monolithic forming may be justified despite the additional construction effort.

checks.title: Whether the alternative introduces disproportionate complexity
checks.description: Blockwork, permanent formwork or separate pours can introduce extra trades, additional concrete pumps, waterproofing detailing or sequencing constraints. In most cases these are simpler to manage than custom monolithic forming, but the alternative should still be assessed against the full construction sequence rather than assumed to always be simpler.

checks.title: Formwork system and contractor capability
checks.description: The thresholds reflect conventional formwork and standard contractor capability. Specialist formwork systems, highly repetitive geometry, experienced formwork crews or project-specific access arrangements may change what is practical, and the construction method should be confirmed with the contractor at the design stage.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a prompt to consider alternatives, not as a rule that monolithic forming above a particular depth is automatically unacceptable. A monolithic fold may still be the most practical solution where there is a specific reason for it; the framework's intent is to make sure that monolithic forming is a deliberate decision rather than an unexamined default.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by formwork productivity literature, contractor consultation, and real project examples where monolithic folds were revised or alternatives were documented.
