evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by supplier availability review, contractor input, practical groundworks logic, and clear reinforcement cage stability and site-adaptability considerations.

relatedEntries.note: Both are about choosing bar sizes that are readily available and can be handled or adjusted on site.

items.name: Rock sockets vs. likely available drilling plant

data.question: Where a rock socket is required, can it realistically be drilled with the plant likely to be available on a job of this type and scale — or does the design quietly assume rock-drilling or piling equipment that a builder is unlikely to bring to site?
data.parameter: The class of drilling plant the required rock socket implies — standard excavator-mounted auger, dedicated rock auger, or piling rig / core barrel — assessed against the plant realistically available for a project of this type and scale. Socket depth, rock strength, hole diameter and the number of repeated sockets are read together to infer the plant class, not read as a depth figure on its own.
data.background:
Once a rock socket is confirmed as necessary (see the related Construction Methods entry), the constructability question is which class of drilling plant the socket implies, and whether that plant is realistically likely on a job of this type and scale. The same socket can be a non-issue on one project and a real problem on another — not because the depth changes, but because the plant the job brings changes. A deep socket on a large job that already has a piling rig on site is routine; a shallower but harder socket on a small job with only an excavator auger can be the bigger problem. So the rating is about the method, not the millimetre count.

A designer cannot confirm what plant a builder will bring — that is a construction decision. What a designer can do is design for the plant realistically likely for the project, and where the design needs more than that, make the requirement explicit rather than leaving it to be discovered at auger refusal.

On small and low-rise projects the likely plant is an excavator, mini-loader or skid-steer with an auger attachment. A standard earth auger suits soil and weak or fractured rock; a rock auger with replaceable teeth can take harder material but may not be part of a contractor's normal kit; a core barrel, casing or restricted-access piling rig is specialist plant. These are different equipment classes, and moving between them changes cost, programme and the access and spoil handling the site must provide.

Two things push a required socket from one plant class to the next: depth (with rock strength and hole diameter) and repetition. A shallow socket near the limit of a standard auger may be manageable, and a slightly deeper one may be tolerated on a job with only two or three piers, where mobilising specialist plant for a handful of holes is uneconomic. The same socket repeated across many piers is a different proposition — a small per-hole difficulty becomes a programme and cost problem, and a dedicated rock auger or piling rig starts to make sense. A builder is unlikely to bring a piling rig to a small job for a few piers, so a design that quietly assumes one risks being discovered only when the auger refuses.

Refusal itself is not always the founding surface. An auger may refuse on a boulder or floater above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives different founding depths across a site, so the actual socket depth — and the plant effort to reach it — may not be known from the drawing. This is why the required socket should be designed against likely plant and made explicit, not left as an assumption.

thresholds.guidance: No rock socket is required, or only a shallow key (around 150 mm or less) into weathered or fractured rock that a standard excavator-mounted auger with the correct teeth can reasonably achieve. This stays within the plant likely available on any site — small or large — subject to geotechnical inspection and founding requirements.

The socket is near the practical limit of a standard auger (around 150 mm), or needs a rock-auger attachment for a small number of piers (say two or three) that a contractor can still manage with an excavator-mounted machine. Do not assume this from the drawing. Design for the rock tooling actually likely on a job this size, and note the rock-drilling requirement so the builder can price and plan for it.

The work is beyond what a standard auger setup should be assumed to drill — a deeper socket (beyond around 200 mm), harder rock, or a larger-diameter hole — or a moderate socket (around 300 mm) is repeated across many piers (say ten or more) so that per-hole difficulty becomes a programme and cost issue. This points to a dedicated rock auger, higher-torque drive or larger machine. Make the required plant explicit before tender, and check it is realistic for the project's scale, access and spoil handling.

The required socket implies a restricted-access piling rig, core barrel or specialist drilling subcontractor — a deep socket, hard competent rock, or many repeated sockets. This is a constructability flag only where that plant is unlikely to be on the job: on a small or low-rise project with a handful of piers, a builder is unlikely to mobilise a piling rig, so the requirement risks being discovered at auger refusal. Where the project already justifies that plant — a larger or deep-foundation job with a piling rig on site — the same socket is routine work, not a concern, and this rating does not apply to you. Where it is not realistic for the job, reconsider the foundation system, reduce the socket requirement, or make the specialist-plant requirement explicit and check it against site access, working platform, headroom, spoil handling and cost before the footing layout is locked in.

alternatives.title: Design for the plant realistically likely on a job of this scale
alternatives.description: A designer cannot confirm the builder's plant, but can match the socket requirement to what is plausible for the project. On a small job with a few piers, assume an excavator-mounted auger or rock auger rather than a piling rig; do not specify a socket that quietly relies on specialist plant a small builder is unlikely to mobilise.

alternatives.title: Keep the socket within standard-auger reach where the design allows
alternatives.description: Where necessity permits, keep the socket shallow or found on cleaned rock so the work stays within the plant likely available. The shallower the required penetration, the more likely it can be achieved with ordinary site plant and the lower the risk of refusal-driven delay.

alternatives.title: Change the foundation system where likely plant cannot reach
alternatives.description: Where the plant likely to be available cannot achieve the required socket, review alternatives such as pads or strips founded on cleaned rock, ground beams spanning between fewer foundations, screw piles where suitable, or fewer larger piles in accessible positions — any geotechnically appropriate arrangement that suits the plant the project can realistically provide.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to bored piers, bored piles, post holes and small foundation excavations where the design requires drilling into rock, especially on residential and low-rise projects where the builder is likely to use an excavator-mounted auger rather than a dedicated piling rig.

checks.title: Plant likely for the project scale
checks.description: The designer cannot confirm the builder's plant, so design against what is realistically likely. A few piers on a small job suggests an excavator auger or rock auger; a large or deep-socket job may justify a piling rig. Match the socket requirement to the plausible plant rather than assuming the most capable equipment is available.

checks.title: Depth as a cue to plant class, not a rating in itself
checks.description: A shallow key into weathered or fractured rock may suit a standard auger; deeper penetration or hard competent rock points to a rock auger or specialist plant. Read the depth together with the geotechnical description and the plant likely for the job — the same depth can sit in different bands on a small job and a large one.

checks.title: Number and repetition of sockets
checks.description: A one-off shallow socket may be handled as a difficult excavation item. The same socket repeated across many piers turns a small per-hole difficulty into a programme and cost problem and can justify a dedicated rock auger or specialist plant.

checks.title: Rock type and strength
checks.description: Weathered or fractured sandstone is a different drilling problem from hard competent rock, basalt or very high-strength material. The plant required depends heavily on rock strength, not only socket depth.

checks.title: Hole diameter
checks.description: A shallow 300 mm hole is a different task from a 450 mm, 600 mm or larger bored pier. Larger diameters increase torque demand, spoil handling, verticality control and the likelihood that piling plant is needed.

checks.title: Access, working platform and spoil handling
checks.description: Rock augers, larger excavators and piling rigs need physical access, working room, suitable ground bearing, overhead clearance, and space for spoil, reinforcement and concrete placement. Restricted access does not necessarily mean standard excavator only — restricted-access piling rigs exist — but they must be planned, not assumed.

checks.title: Refusal, floaters and sloping rock
checks.description: Refusal is not always the founding surface. An auger may refuse on a boulder or floater above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives variable founding depths, so socket depth and the plant effort to reach it may not be knowable from the drawing.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a constructability prompt about the plant the socket implies. Locate the entry by the class of plant the required socket needs and whether that plant is realistic for a job of this type and scale — not by the depth figure on its own. A deep socket on a job that already justifies a piling rig is routine; a shallow-but-hard socket on a small job with only an excavator auger can be the bigger problem. The aim is to design the required socket against the plant realistically available and to make the requirement explicit — not to confirm the builder's plant, which the designer cannot do.

data.evidence:
Standard excavator/post-hole auger envelope. Hire-style post-hole attachments for mini-loaders and similar plant are marketed for shallow foundation and post-hole work, with depths around 2 m when an extension is used. This frames the ordinary small-site augering envelope against which a rock socket requirement should be checked.

Standard augers are not hard-rock tools. Specialist manufacturers distinguish rock augers from standard augers. Digga describes tapered rock augers as extreme rock-drilling tools that reach ground unachievable with a standard auger. A normal earth auger should not be assumed to drill meaningful sockets into hard rock.

Specialist piling uses different tooling. Bored-pile guidance treats augers, rock augers, buckets, casing and core barrels as separate tools selected for ground conditions. Soilmec identifies the core barrel as the suitable tool for very hard rock. Keller describes bored piles from around 450 mm diameter upward across a wide range of soil and rock. This supports the distinction between excavator augering and specialist bored-pile/rock-drilling practice.

Rock drilling is slower and costlier, and refusal is not always founding. NCHRP Synthesis 360 notes that drilling and excavation in rock is generally more expensive and time-consuming than in soil and may require specialised techniques, equipment and experience, and distinguishes rock augers, core barrels and downhole hammers as different tool classes. It also illustrates that an auger may refuse on a boulder above true bedrock and that sloping rock produces variable founding depth — so socket depth, and the plant to reach it, cannot be read off the drawing alone.

Band basis. The bands are keyed to plant class — standard auger, dedicated rock auger, and piling rig / core barrel — a distinction the equipment and piling references above support directly. The depth, rock-strength and pier-count cues used to place a socket into a plant class (around 150 mm for a standard auger, up to around 200 mm on a job with only a few piers, around 300 mm repeated across many piers pointing to a rock auger, and deeper or harder rock pointing to specialist plant) are practical design-stage prompts based on contractor and project knowledge, not code or manufacturer limits. They should be refined with regional contractor feedback and project examples.

References
Kennards Hire. Mini Loader Post Hole Attachment. Accessed 2026.
Digga Australia. Tapered Rock Augers. Accessed 2026.
Soilmec. Bored Piles. Accessed 2026.
Keller Australia. Bored Piles. Accessed 2026.
Turner, J. (2006). Rock-Socketed Shafts for Highway Structure Foundations. NCHRP Synthesis 360. Transportation Research Board.
Virnig Manufacturing. Auger Bits product page. Source for standard and rock auger comparison image.
ToolTuff Direct. Tool Tuff Earth Ogre Industrial-Duty Rock Auger product page. Source for rock auger cutting teeth image.

supportingImages.title:
Standard auger compared with rock auger
Standard and rock auger bits may fit the same general drive system, but they are intended for different ground conditions. Source: Virnig Manufacturing.

Why drilled depth is uncertain: refusal, floaters and sloping rock
An auger may refuse on a boulder above true bedrock, and sloping rock gives different founding depths across a site. Required socket depth — and the plant needed to achieve it — cannot be read off the drawing alone. Source: NCHRP Synthesis 360 (Turner, 2006).

Rock auger cutting teeth
Rock augers use specialised cutting heads and replaceable teeth for harder material. Source: ToolTuff Direct.

evidenceSupport.summary: The bands are keyed to plant class — standard auger, rock auger, core barrel and bored-pile rig — a distinction supported directly by equipment and piling references (Kennards, Digga, Soilmec, Keller) and by NCHRP practice guidance on rock-drilling cost and refusal, with product images of standard versus rock auger tooling. The depth, rock-strength and pier-count cues used to assign a socket to a plant class are practitioner/project judgement rather than published limits and should be refined with regional contractor feedback.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Construction Methods entry. Decide there whether the socket is necessary; this entry covers whether the required socket can be drilled with the plant likely available on the site.
Both concern whether the design quietly assumes plant, attachments or access that may not be part of the normal construction setup — check equipment and access together.
Both concern foundation system selection where available plant, access and ground conditions can change the practical construction method.
Both concern the constructability consequences of the chosen pier or pile detailing method.

items.name: Slab bar diameter and on-site bending equipment

data.question: Where slab reinforcement uses bars greater than 16 mm diameter, has the design recognised that any on-site bending of those bars is unlikely to be handled with ordinary low-cost site bending tools?
data.parameter: Nominal slab reinforcement diameter and implied on-site bending equipment class.
data.background:
The detailing entry linked to this one asks whether slab reinforcement can be kept to 12 mm or 16 mm bars where structurally practical. This logistics entry explains why that matters on site.

The issue is not cutting. Bars can often be cut on site using grinders, saws or other cutting methods. The real logistics change occurs when bars need to be bent. SRIA guidance places ordinary manual bending tools at up to about 16 mm diameter. Above that size, cold bending requires considerably more force and generally moves into powered or specialist bending equipment.

This matters because 16 mm bending equipment is still within normal site-tool territory. A simple manual lever cutter/bender is relatively low-cost and portable. A portable powered bender is more expensive, but the example reviewed is still only rated to 16 mm and is mainly useful where bars are already fixed or cast in, such as starters projecting from a wall that need to be bent down into a slab.

Once bending moves into 20 mm, 24 mm, 32 mm or larger bars, the equipment class changes sharply. The tools become larger, heavier, more expensive and much less likely to be casually available on a normal site. In practice, larger bars are far more likely to be bent off site by the reinforcement supplier or processor. That makes them much less adaptable once they arrive on site.

This entry is not saying that 20 mm or larger slab bars should be avoided where they are structurally required. The design engineer chooses bar diameter and spacing. The logistics prompt is that using larger bars has a consequence: if the detail later needs bending or local adaptation, the site may not have realistic equipment to do it. For general slab reinforcement, this is another reason to use 12 mm and 16 mm bars where structurally practical, and to keep larger bars localised to where they are genuinely needed.

thresholds.guidance: Slab reinforcement remains within the ordinary 16 mm site bending equipment range. These bars can be handled with simple manual lever benders or portable powered tools where proper bending equipment is used. This gives the reinforcement higher on-site adaptability and reduces reliance on off-site bending for minor changes.

Slab reinforcement uses bars greater than 16 mm diameter. These bars are outside the ordinary manual lever-bender range and, if they need to be bent on site, generally require powered or specialist bending equipment. This equipment is much more expensive and less likely to be casually available on site. Larger bars may still be structurally necessary, but they should be treated as low-adaptability site items and not used broadly where 12 mm or 16 mm bars would work.

alternatives.title: Keep general slab reinforcement within the 16 mm bending-equipment range where structurally practical
alternatives.description: Where 12 mm or 16 mm bars can achieve the design, prefer those sizes for general slab reinforcement. This keeps the detail within the ordinary site bending equipment class and avoids unnecessary reliance on off-site bending or specialist tools.

alternatives.title: Treat 20 mm and larger bars as low-adaptability site items
alternatives.description: Do not assume bars greater than 16 mm can be conveniently bent or adjusted on site. If 20 mm or larger bars are used, the design should assume they will usually arrive in their final required shape or be used straight, rather than relying on casual site bending.

alternatives.title: Keep larger bars localised to genuine demand
alternatives.description: Where 20 mm or larger bars are structurally required, keep them to the strips, bands, supports, transfer zones or local details where the demand exists. Use 12 mm or 16 mm bars elsewhere where structurally practical.

alternatives.title: Do not confuse cutting with bending
alternatives.description: The logistics problem is bending, not simply cutting. A site may be able to cut larger bars with grinders or saws, but bending those bars accurately and safely is a different task and usually requires a different class of equipment.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to slab reinforcement where the designer is choosing bar diameter and spacing, especially where a choice exists between smaller bars at closer spacing and larger bars at wider spacing.

checks.title: Relevant slab types
checks.description: This entry is most relevant to suspended slabs, raft slabs, podium slabs, balconies, setdowns, slab thickenings and local reinforcement zones where reinforcement may need to be coordinated with site conditions.

checks.title: Larger bars may still be structurally required
checks.description: This entry does not apply where larger bars are structurally required and are deliberately detailed for that purpose. It is not a rule against 20 mm, 24 mm, 32 mm or larger reinforcement.

checks.title: Logistics prompt, not a method statement
checks.description: This entry does not ask the design engineer to nominate the contractor’s bending tool, bending method or construction procedure. The engineer controls bar diameter and spacing. The contractor controls means and methods. The constructability issue is that the engineer’s bar-size choice can shift the work from ordinary site tooling to specialist or off-site bending.

checks.title: Bending is the issue, not cutting
checks.description: A site may still cut larger bars with grinders, saws or other cutting methods. The equipment-class jump discussed in this entry is about bending bars accurately and safely, not merely cutting them to length.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a logistics prompt about the equipment class implied by the bar diameter. Bars greater than 16 mm are not prohibited, but they should be treated as lower-adaptability site items unless suitable bending equipment or off-site fabrication has been allowed for by the construction team.

data.evidence:
SRIA guidance identifies 16 mm as the practical hinge for on-site bending. SRIA states that manual bending tools are recommended for bars up to about 16 mm diameter, with electric bending tools recommended for larger bars. SRIA Technical Note 4 also states that cold bending bars greater than 16 mm requires considerable force, that 12 mm and 16 mm bars should be bent cold, and that 20 mm and larger bars preferably require power-operated bending tools.

The reviewed tool examples support the logistics consequence of that hinge. A simple manual lever cutter/bender rated to 16 mm was shown at $509.95. A portable powered bender rated to 4–16 mm was shown at $1,798.50. However, moving into larger bar bending equipment caused a major cost and equipment-class jump: an industrial 6–32 mm electric bender/cutter was shown at $12,618.00, and a heavy-duty 6–46 mm bender was shown at $36,995.00 and required 400–415 V power.

This supports the entry’s constructability conclusion: 12 mm and 16 mm slab bars remain within ordinary site bending equipment territory, while 20 mm and larger bars should be treated as low-adaptability items that are more likely to require off-site bending or specialist equipment if bending is needed.

References
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2016). Bending Reinforcement on Site (Issue No. 42/4). SRIA.
Steel Reinforcement Institute of Australia. (2007). Technical Note 4: Fabrication and Site Handling of Reinforcing Bars. SRIA.
Sydney Tools. (2026). HK Porter 0590RBJN 1320mm/52" Rebar Cutter & Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL ERB-16 4mm–16mm Rebar Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL CRBC-32A 6mm–32mm Electric Industrial Rebar Bender & Cutter. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.
Sydney Tools. (2026). RAPIDTOOL PRB-46X Premium 400–415V Heavy Duty 6–46mm Rebar Bender. Product page screenshot reviewed June 2026.

A simple lever-type cutter and bender is low-cost site equipment, shown here at $509.95. It represents the ordinary bending-tool class available for smaller bars. SRIA guidance places manual bending tools at up to about 16 mm diameter, which aligns with the practical 12/16 mm slab reinforcement range. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Portable powered rebar bender — up to 16 mm
A portable powered bender is a more expensive equipment class, shown here at $1,798.50, but this example is still limited to 4–16 mm bars. It may be useful where bars are already fixed or cast in, such as 16 mm starters projecting from a wall that need to be bent into a slab. It does not solve the 20 mm and larger bar-bending issue. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Once bending moves into the 20–32 mm range, the equipment class changes dramatically. This 6–32 mm electric industrial bender/cutter is shown at $12,618.00, far above ordinary 16 mm site tools. This supports treating 20 mm and larger bars as low-adaptability site items unless specialist equipment or off-site bending is planned. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

Heavy-duty rebar bender — up to 46 mm
Large-capacity bending equipment is a specialist plant item rather than normal site tooling. This 6–46 mm heavy-duty bender is shown at $36,995.00 and requires 400–415 V power. At this equipment class, the bar is effectively a planned or fabricated item rather than something expected to be conveniently adapted on site. Source: Sydney Tools product page screenshot.

evidenceSupport.summary: The 16 mm equipment hinge is directly supported by SRIA guidance, and the reviewed tool examples clearly show the cost and equipment-class jump from 16 mm tools to larger-bar bending equipment. The exact cost ratios are based on a small set of current supplier screenshots and should be treated as illustrative rather than universal.

relatedEntries.note: Companion Detailing entry. Decide there whether 20 mm and larger slab bars are genuinely required; this entry explains the equipment and availability consequences if those larger bars need to be bent on site.
Both concern how reinforcement size choices affect site adaptability, ordinary equipment availability and the likelihood of substitution or delay.

items.name: Equipment access constraints

data.question: Has the foundation / element been chosen so it can be installed with equipment that can actually reach and operate on this site — for example a pile type an excavator-mounted attachment can install — rather than one that assumes a large dedicated rig the site cannot accommodate or afford to mobilise?
data.parameter: Match between the installation equipment a design implies (e.g. dedicated piling rig) and what the site can realistically accommodate (access, headroom, mobilisation), including whether an excavator-based method is viable.
data._evidenceAngle: Logistics root cause: design commits the site to equipment it can't get in or justify. Direct link to the screw-pile entry — screw piles can be installed with an excavator, avoiding a dedicated rig (industry sources). Use installer/contractor practice sources for the equipment envelopes (access width, headroom, mobilisation cost), the way the off-form entry uses formwork working envelopes.
data._thresholdSketch: Good: installable with equipment the site can accommodate. Consider: feasible but constrained access. Concern: assumes equipment that barely fits / costly to mobilise. Action required: implied equipment cannot access the site as designed.

relatedEntries.note: Both depend on whether construction plant and workers can physically reach and work the element on this site — check site access together.

items.name: Slab depth consistency

data.question: Do the suspended slabs share a consistent depth across the floor and up the building — letting one set of formwork and shoring cycle and reshore cleanly — or do varying depths fragment the formwork system and reshoring into many one-off setups?
data.parameter: Number of distinct suspended-slab depths across the floor plate and up the building.
data._evidenceAngle: Buildability + reuse, no standards content. Consistent depth lets a single formwork/shoring set cycle and reshore (ties to the PT formwork-cycling entry and the learning-curve entry). Practice references: formwork/shoring cycling literature on reshore stacking needing matching levels. Pairs naturally with the repetition entry.
data._thresholdSketch: Good: one (or few) slab depths. Consider: a couple of depths. Concern: many depths fragmenting formwork/reshoring. Action required: depth varies so much a cycling system can't be sustained.

relatedEntries.note: Both affect how economically formwork can be reused. The more the design standardises and repeats, the more formwork investment pays off — consider them together.
Both affect how economically formwork can be reused. The more the design standardises and repeats, the more formwork investment pays off — consider them together.

items.name: Concrete grade specification

data.question: How many different concrete grades has the design specified across the project — is the number kept low enough for clean ordering, batching and placement, or do many closely-spaced grades multiply supply, QA and wrong-pour risk for little structural gain?
data.parameter: Number of distinct concrete grades specified across the project relative to need.
data._evidenceAngle: Logistics/buildability: each extra grade is a separate order, batch, delivery and test stream, and a wrong-pour opportunity, often for marginal structural benefit. Standards in the back of mind (don't drop below what an element needs); the point is rationalising the count. Practice references: ready-mix supply / site QA literature on order and placement error with many mixes; corroborate with error-rate / variety literature.
data._thresholdSketch: Good: few, well-separated grades. Consider: several grades. Concern: many closely-spaced grades. Action required: grade proliferation with high wrong-pour risk and little structural justification.

dimensions.name: Safety in Design
dimensions.description: Design decisions affecting the safety of construction workers and other stakeholders
generalGuidance: Consider the top reinforcement mat as a temporary working platform — its spacing directly affects worker safety during concrete placement
Every detail must be physically executable — if a worker cannot safely access, reach, or work in the space the detail requires, the design must be reconsidered

items.name: Reinforcement mat fall-through risk

data.question: At what top reinforcement spacing does the risk of a worker falling through or stepping between bars during construction become unacceptable?
data.parameter: Top reinforcement bar spacing (mm)
data.background: Top reinforcement mats in slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements temporarily serve as working surfaces during construction until concrete is placed. When bar spacing is too wide, workers risk stepping between bars, leading to falls and injuries. The spacing of the top mat directly determines whether it can safely support foot traffic during concrete placement and finishing. Any measures to address fall-through risk must also facilitate concrete placement and not obstruct the pour.

thresholds.guidance: Top mat provides adequate support as a working platform. Workers can walk and kneel on the reinforcement with minimal risk of stepping through. This is the recommended maximum spacing where top mats will be used as working platforms.

Fall-through risk on larger pours and should be addressed. The exception is small, single-truck pours under approximately 7 m3, where the pour is self-paced and conditions are controlled enough that 250 mm spacing is acceptable. For example, if the slab thickness is 180 mm, this equates to slabs less than approximately 38 m2. For pours exceeding 7 m3 (i.e. requiring more than one truck), this spacing should be addressed.

Unacceptable fall-through risk for any mat intended as a working platform. Spacing this wide provides almost no resistance to a misplaced step. 300 mm is the maximum spacing permitted under AS 3600, so greater spacings should not be encountered. If 300 x 300 is specified, it must be addressed before construction proceeds.

alternatives.title: Specify 200 x 200 mm top reinforcement spacing
alternatives.description: Use 200 x 200 mm spacing, or closer, as the preferred solution where the top mat will be used as a working surface during concrete placement. This provides a more supportive surface for workers and avoids relying on supplementary controls.

alternatives.title: Assess 250 x 250 mm spacing against pour size and site conditions
alternatives.description: Where 250 x 250 mm spacing is proposed, consider the concrete pour volume and construction context. Small pours under approximately 7 m3 are generally more controlled and may be acceptable, while larger pours are more likely to involve faster movement, more workers and higher exposure time.

alternatives.title: Use supplementary top mesh or reduce the primary spacing
alternatives.description: Where 250 x 250 mm spacing is used on pours exceeding approximately 7 m3, or where 300 x 300 mm spacing is specified, provide an additional layer of top mesh such as SL82 or above to improve the working surface, or revise the primary top reinforcement spacing to 200 x 200 mm. Consider cover, reinforcement congestion and concrete placement before adding supplementary mesh.

data.evidence:
Site incident report: worker sustained a severe knee injury after stepping through 250 x 250 top reinforcement during slab pour. Supported by Safe Work Australia guidance on falls through openings and gaps in working platforms. Boot sole dimensions derived from ADF anthropometric surveys (Blanchonette, 2017). Slip and trip injury data from construction site research demonstrates that even non-fall slip events predominantly injure lower extremities (Lipscomb et al., 2006). Prevention through Design guidance for concrete construction recommends mat mesh over widely spaced rebar as a walking surface control (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, 2024).

Pour size threshold (7 m3)
The 7 m3 threshold corresponds to the typical capacity of a single concrete truck. Pours within a single truck are generally self-paced and controlled, because the rate of placement is set by the workers screeding and finishing rather than by trucks queueing on site. Pours requiring multiple trucks introduce time pressure to keep up with placement and faster worker movement across the reinforcement mat, increasing exposure time and the likelihood of a misplaced step.

References
Blanchonette, P. (2017). Australian Defence Force Anthropometry: A Summary of Historic Surveys (DST-Group-GD-0968). Defence Science and Technology Group.
Lipscomb, H. J., Glazner, J. E., Bondy, J., Guarini, K., & Lezotte, D. (2006). Injuries from slips and trips in construction. Applied Ergonomics, 37(3), 267–274. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apergo.2005.07.008
National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health. (2024). Prevention through Design Toolkit for the Construction Industry (DHHS (NIOSH) Publication No. 2024-124). U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. https://doi.org/10.26616/NIOSHPUB2024124

examples.caption: Top reinforcement at N12-250 spacing on a 14.26 m3 pour. While the matching top and bottom spacing simplifies detailing, the 250 mm top mat spacing creates unacceptable fall-through risk on a pour of this size.
Top reinforcement at N12-250 spacing on a small balcony pour of approximately 3.8 m3. The small pour volume means conditions are controlled and the 250 mm spacing is acceptable.

Top reinforcement at N12-200 spacing on a 12.7 m3 pour. Bottom mat at N12-300 is not a concern as it is not used as a working platform. The 200 mm top spacing provides a safe working surface during concrete placement.

At an angled approach, 250 mm spacing presents a step-through risk for both 5th percentile and mean boot sizes.

applicability.appliesWhere: This entry applies to top reinforcement mats in slabs and similar reinforced concrete elements where workers are expected to walk, kneel or stand on the reinforcement during concrete placement, vibration, screeding or finishing. This includes suspended slabs, slabs-on-ground, raft slabs, balconies, podium slabs and other reinforced concrete pours where the top mat functions as a temporary working surface.

checks.title: Top mat used as a working surface
checks.description: This guidance is primarily concerned with the top reinforcement mat because this is the layer workers walk, kneel or stand on during concrete placement. Bottom reinforcement, wall reinforcement, starter bars and other non-trafficable reinforcement do not create the same working-surface risk. However, designers may still choose to match top and bottom reinforcement spacing where this improves detailing simplicity, ordering, placement consistency or reduces the likelihood of site errors.

checks.title: Pour volume is a practical indicator, not a fixed safety boundary
checks.description: The 7 m3 threshold is used as a practical indicator of pour intensity and exposure, not as a statutory or absolute safety limit. A smaller pour may still require closer spacing if access is awkward, the reinforcement is difficult to move across, or workers need to spend extended time on the mat.

checks.title: Structural and placement requirements remain controlling
checks.description: Any change to reinforcement spacing or addition of supplementary mesh must still satisfy structural design requirements, cover, bar layering, congestion, concrete placement and vibration requirements.

applicability.note: Use the rating as a safety-in-design review prompt, not as a blanket rule. The appropriate response is to consider whether the top mat will function as a working surface and whether the specified spacing creates an avoidable risk during construction.

evidenceSupport.summary: Supported by safety-in-design literature, anthropometric/boot-size reasoning, practical site experience, and a clear worker fall-through mechanism.
